title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
This study applies a systematic ARIMA time series modelling workflow to the weekly adjusted closing price of Commonwealth Bank of Australia (CBA.AX) — one of Australia’s “Big Four” banks and the largest company by market capitalisation on the ASX 200. CBA’s price movements are closely watched as a barometer of the broader Australian financial sector, domestic housing market conditions, and the Reserve Bank of Australia’s (RBA) monetary policy trajectory.
The analysis follows:

Exploratory Data Analysis — visual inspection of trend, variance, and distributional properties.
Transformation — Box-Cox variance stabilisation where warranted.
Differencing & Stationarity — achieving a time-invariant mean via differencing, verified by formal unit-root tests.
Model Specification — AR and MA order identification via ACF, PACF, EACF, and BIC tables.
Model Fitting & Residual Diagnostics — CSS and ML estimation with full residual analysis and overparameterised model checks.
Forecasting — producing point forecasts and prediction intervals using multiple methods.

title: So What?
text:
Australia has long been the world’s dominant supplier of fine wool. Today, wool and sheepskin exports contribute more than AU$3.5 billion annually to the national economy, sustaining thousands of farming families across New South Wales, Victoria, and Western Australia. But beneath that headline figure lies a commodity story defined by dramatic boom-and-bust cycles, structural shocks, and persistent seasonal rhythms that have shaped, and at times devastated, the industry.
The most defining moment in modern wool market history came in February 1991, when the Australian Wool Corporation’s Reserve Price Scheme collapsed. For years, the scheme had artificially propped up wool prices by guaranteeing a floor price and stockpiling surplus product. When the stockpile became financially unsustainable, the scheme was abandoned overnight. Prices fell by nearly 50% within months. Farmers destocked, regional communities contracted, and the industry spent most of the 1990s in structural decline. This single event permanently altered the long-term trajectory of Australia’s wool trade — a “before and after” line that any serious analysis of the data must account for.
Within that broader arc, the wool trade also follows a deeply seasonal rhythm. Australia’s wool clip, the annual shearing cycle, drives predictable surges in export activity during the December–March period as freshly shorn wool moves through the supply chain. Trade values reliably trough around August, before recovering through the second half of the year. This within-year pattern has held consistently for decades, making seasonality one of the most exploitable structural features of the series for forecasting purposes.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
1.1 Define the ASX Top 10 Tickers
The tidyquant function tq_exchange() only supports US exchanges (AMEX, NASDAQ, NYSE) and cannot retrieve an ASX constituent list. Instead, we manually specify the top 30 ASX stocks by market capitalisation — the most liquid, most influential companies on the Australian market. Together they represent the core of the ASX 200’s total market cap weight.
Yahoo Finance requires the .AX suffix for all ASX-listed stocks (e.g. CBA → CBA.AX). We store both the clean ASX symbol and the Yahoo Finance symbol for clarity.


Code
# Manually define the top 30 ASX stocks by market cap.
# Three columns are created:
#   symbol    — the official ASX ticker (used for display and filtering)
#   company   — full company name (used in tables and labels)
#   sector    — GICS sector classification (enables sector-level breakdowns later)
# A fourth column symbol_yf is derived by appending ".AX" for Yahoo Finance.

asx10_tickers <- tibble(
  symbol  = c(
    "BHP",  "CBA",  "CSL",  "NAB",  "WBC",
    "ANZ",  "WES",  "MQG",  "RIO",  "TLS"
  ),
  company = c(
    "BHP Group",               "Commonwealth Bank",       "CSL Limited",
    "National Australia Bank", "Westpac Banking",         "ANZ Banking Group",
    "Wesfarmers",              "Macquarie Group",         "Rio Tinto",
    "Telstra"
  ),
  sector  = c(
    "Materials",   "Financials",  "Healthcare",
    "Financials",  "Financials",  "Financials",
    "Consumer",    "Financials",  "Materials",
    "Telecom"
  )
) %>%
  mutate(symbol_yf = paste0(symbol, ".AX"))

section: 1.3 Time Series Plots — Price Level & Volumes
text:
1.3 Time Series Plots — Price Level & Volumes
For this we choose Commonwealth Bank of Australia (CBA), one of Australia’s “Big Four” banks and a key component of the ASX 10 index, as a example. Before fitting any forecasting model, we must understand the underlying structure of our series. The first diagnostic step is always a visual inspection. Time series decomposition aims to separate a series into three fundamental components:

Trend: Long-term directional movement (upward/downward/stationary)
Seasonality: Regular periodic fluctuations (patterns)
Change Point
Changing Variance
Behavior



Code
# First filter for CBA stock only
cba_data <- asx10_prices_raw %>%
  filter(symbol == "CBA.AX") %>%
  arrange(date)  # Ensure chronological order

# Now create ts objects for CBA
cba_prices_ts <- ts(
  cba_data$adjusted,
  start = c(2024, 4),  # April 2024
  frequency = 365
)

cba_volumes_ts <- ts(
  cba_data$volume,
  start = c(2024, 4),
  frequency = 365
)


# Two-panel layout: price level on top, returns on bottom.
par(mfrow = c(2, 1), cex = 0.85)

plot(cba_prices_ts,
     type = "l",  # "l" for line (better than "o" for daily data)
     ylab = "Price",
     main = "CBA Daily Adjusted Price")

plot(cba_volumes_ts,
     type = "l",
     ylab = "Volumes",
     main = "CBA Daily Volumes")

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
section: 2.3 Isolate CBA and Construct Time Series Objects
text:
2.3 Isolate CBA and Construct Time Series Objects
We select Commonwealth Bank (CBA.AX) as the focus of this analysis. CBA is well-suited for a univariate ARIMA investigation because it is highly liquid (minimal missing data), has a long and stable listing history, and its price trajectory reflects the interaction of macroeconomic conditions, monetary policy (RBA interest rate decisions), and domestic housing market dynamics — all of which produce rich autocorrelative structure worth modelling.


Code
# Filter and sort to ensure strictly chronological ordering
cba_data <- asx10_prices_raw %>%
  filter(symbol == "CBA.AX") %>%
  arrange(date)

# Create ts objects.
# Daily financial data does not have a perfectly integer frequency, but we use
# frequency = 252 (approximate annual trading days) to retain temporal awareness.
# All modelling is performed on the log-transformed adjusted price (see Section 3).
cba_prices_ts <- ts(
  cba_data$adjusted,
  start     = c(2024, 1),
  frequency = 252         # approx trading days per year
)

cba_volumes_ts <- ts(
  cba_data$volume,
  start     = c(2024, 1),
  frequency = 252
)

cat("CBA observations:", length(cba_prices_ts), "\n")


CBA observations: 508

    "to", format(max(cba_data$date), "%d %b %Y"), "\n")

Code

    "–", round(max(cba_data$adjusted), 2), "\n")

        main = "CBA Daily Adjusted Closing Price",
        ylab = "Price (AUD)")





CBA.AX adjusted close price. The price level drifts upward with no stable mean — a clearly non-stationary series. Volume exhibits occasional spikes consistent with earnings announcements and major macro events.

section: 3.1 Time Series Plot — Price Level and Volume
text:
3.1 Time Series Plot — Price Level and Volume


Code
# Two-panel layout: price level on top, volume on bottom.
# These are the most fundamental visual diagnostics — they tell us immediately
# whether the series has a stable mean (stationarity) and stable variance.
par(mfrow = c(2, 1), cex = 0.85)

plot_ts(cba_prices_ts,
        main = "CBA Daily Adjusted Closing Price",
        ylab = "Price (AUD)")

plot_ts(cba_volumes_ts,
        main = "CBA Daily Trading Volume",
        ylab = "Volume (shares)")





CBA.AX adjusted close price (top) and daily trading volume (bottom). The price level drifts upward with no stable mean — a clearly non-stationary series. Volume exhibits occasional spikes consistent with earnings announcements and major macro events.




Code
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))


The price plot reveals a sustained upward trend over the two-year observation window. The level shifts progressively higher with no horizontal mean, which is the hallmark signature of a non-stationary series requiring differencing before ARIMA modelling. The volume series is more erratic, with episodic spikes that likely correspond to key ASX events such as RBA interest rate decisions, CBA earnings announcements, and macro shocks. For this analysis, we focus exclusively on the adjusted price series.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
3.2 Distribution and Decomposition
This step follows the Module 7 / ARIMAX workflow EDA section. We examine the raw distribution of the training series alongside a classical multiplicative decomposition, which separates the series into trend, seasonal, and irregular components.


Code
# We work on a training subsample (hold out the last 30 trading days for forecast
# evaluation), consistent with standard time series modelling practice.
n_train <- length(cba_prices_ts) - 30
y_train <- window(cba_prices_ts, end = time(cba_prices_ts)[n_train])
y_test  <- window(cba_prices_ts, start = time(cba_prices_ts)[n_train + 1])

cat("Training observations:", length(y_train), "\n")


Training observations: 478 


Code
cat("Test observations (forecast evaluation):", length(y_test), "\n\n")


Test observations (forecast evaluation): 30 


Code
# ── EDA Panel ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))

# Panel 1: Raw series with mean reference line
plot(y_train, main = "Raw CBA Price with Mean Line", 
     ylab = "Price (AUD)", xlab = "Time",lwd = 1, type = "l")
abline(h = mean(y_train), col = "red", lty = 2)

# Panel 2: 21-day moving average
if (length(y_train) >= 21) {
  ma_window <- 21
  ma_centered <- stats::filter(as.numeric(y_train), 
                                rep(1/ma_window, ma_window), 
                                sides = 2)
  ma_ts <- ts(ma_centered, start = start(y_train), frequency = frequency(y_train))
  plot(y_train, main = "CBA Price with 21-day Moving Average",
       ylab = "Price (AUD)", xlab = "Time", type = "l", lwd = 1)
  lines(ma_ts, col = "red", lwd = 1.5)
} else {
  plot(y_train, main = "CBA Price (Insufficient data for MA)",
       ylab = "Price (AUD)", xlab = "Time", type = "l")
}

# Panel 3: QQ plot of daily returns
qqnorm(y_train, main = "QQ Plot — CBA Daily Price", 
       pch = 16, cex = 0.7)
qqline(y_train, col = "red", lty = 2, lwd = 1.5)

# Panel 4: Histogram of daily returns with normal curve
hist(y_train, breaks = 20, 
     main = "Histogram — Daily Returns",
     xlab = "Price (AUD)", 
     border = "white", freq = TRUE)
curve(dnorm(x, mean = mean(y_train, na.rm = TRUE),
            sd = sd(y_train, na.rm = TRUE)) * length(y_train) *
        diff(hist(y_train, plot = FALSE, breaks = 20)$breaks)[1],
      add = TRUE, col = "red", lwd = 2)





EDA panel for CBA adjusted price: (top-left) raw series with mean reference; (top-right) 21-day moving average; (bottom-left) QQ plot of daily returns; (bottom-right) histogram of daily returns.




Code
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))


The EDA panel confirms several key structural properties of the CBA price series:

Raw series with mean reference: The price level climbs steadily from approximately AUD 105 to over AUD 150 during the training window. The series never reverts to the sample mean — the red dashed reference line lies in the middle of the range, but the actual trajectory drifts persistently above and below it without mean-reversion. This is definitive visual evidence of non-stationarity in the mean, which will require differencing.
Distribution: The histogram reveals a multi-modal, near-uniform spread across the price range, reflecting the fact that a trending series visits all price levels roughly equally during its journey upward. This is not a problem for modelling — it simply confirms that we are looking at a random walk with drift rather than a stationary series.
Decomposition: The trend component confirms the sustained upward drift. The seasonal component (operating at the 252-day frequency, capturing any weekly or intra-annual trading patterns) shows modest regular variation. The irregular component displays the day-to-day fluctuations after removing trend and seasonality — these should behave like white noise if the decomposition has adequately captured the signal.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
section: 3.2 ACF and PACF of the Raw Series
text:
3.2 ACF and PACF of the Raw Series


Code
# ACF/PACF of the raw price series (before any transformation or differencing).
# These plots provide the first formal evidence of the autocorrelative structure.
plot_acf_pacf(
  y_train,
  acf_title  = "ACF — CBA Raw Price",
  pacf_title = "PACF — CBA Raw Price"
)





ACF and PACF of the raw CBA adjusted closing price. The slowly decaying ACF is the textbook signature of a non-stationary series with a stochastic trend.





ACF (slow linear decay): The autocorrelations remain large and positive across many lags, declining so slowly that they barely approach zero within the plotted range. This slow decay pattern is the canonical diagnostic for a unit-root (non-stationary) process — the series has memory that persists indefinitely. For CBA, this reflects the momentum in Australian bank equity prices: each day’s price is strongly predicted by the previous day’s price because institutional forces (housing market trends, RBA policy cycles) mean that price movements tend to persist rather than revert.
PACF (single dominant spike at lag 1): The PACF drops to near-zero after the first lag. This is characteristic of an AR(1)-type process, where only the immediately preceding observation carries direct predictive power once the chain of autocorrelations is accounted for. This pattern is consistent with a random walk — a special case of AR(1) with the autoregressive coefficient equal to 1.

Evidence collected: Both the visual trend in the time series plot and the slow ACF decay confirm that the raw CBA price series is non-stationary. We must apply at least one round of differencing before any ARIMA model can be fitted.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
4.1 Box-Cox Analysis
Before differencing, we assess whether a variance-stabilising transformation is warranted. For daily stock prices, the variance of returns tends to be proportional to the price level (heteroscedasticity) — a higher-priced stock fluctuates more in dollar terms but roughly the same in percentage terms. The log transformation is the natural choice for financial data: it converts multiplicative price movements into additive log-returns and stabilises variance.
We use BoxCox.ar() from the TSA package to formally estimate the optimal Box-Cox parameter λ. The function fits AR models across a grid of λ values and selects the one that maximises the log-likelihood, with a 95% confidence interval.


Code
# BoxCox.ar() fits AR models across a grid of lambda values.
# It returns the lambda that maximises the profile log-likelihood and a
# 95% confidence interval. If the CI includes lambda = 0, a log transform is
# supported; if it includes lambda = 1, no transformation is needed.
BC <- BoxCox.ar(y = y_train, lambda = seq(-1, 1.5, 0.01))


Warning in arima0(x, order = c(i, 0L, 0L), include.mean = demean): possible
convergence problem: optim gave code = 1





Box-Cox log-likelihood profile for CBA price series. The confidence interval for the optimal λ and its relationship to λ = 0 (log) and λ = 1 (no transform) determines whether transformation is necessary.




Code
BC$ci


[1] -0.83  0.19


Code
lambda_opt   <- BC$lambda[which.max(BC$loglike)]
lambda_lower <- BC$ci[1]
lambda_upper <- BC$ci[2]

cat("=== Box-Cox Results ===\n")


=== Box-Cox Results ===


Code
cat(sprintf("  Optimal lambda    : %.4f\n", lambda_opt))


  Optimal lambda    : -0.3300


Code
cat(sprintf("  95%% CI           : [%.4f, %.4f]\n", lambda_lower, lambda_upper))


  95% CI           : [-0.8300, 0.1900]


Code
cat(sprintf("  Does CI include 0 (log)?  %s\n",
            ifelse(lambda_lower <= 0 & lambda_upper >= 0, "YES", "NO")))


  Does CI include 0 (log)?  YES


Code
cat(sprintf("  Does CI include 1 (none)? %s\n",
            ifelse(lambda_lower <= 1 & lambda_upper >= 1, "YES", "NO")))


  Does CI include 1 (none)? NO


Interpretation: The optimal λ is close to zero, and the 95% confidence interval typically includes λ = 0 for financial price data. This provides formal statistical support for the log transformation, which is also theoretically motivated: log-returns are the standard quantity in financial econometrics because they are additive across time, bounded below by −1 (no more than 100% loss), and approximately normally distributed over short horizons. We therefore proceed with the log-transformed price series for all subsequent modelling.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
4.2 Apply Log Transformation


Code
# Apply log transformation to the training series.
# log(price) is the continuously compounded price level. Its first difference
# gives the daily log-return: Δ log(P_t) = log(P_t) − log(P_{t−1}) ≈ r_t
log_train <- log(y_train)
log_test  <- log(y_test)

par(mfrow = c(2, 1), cex = 0.85)

plot_ts(y_train, main = "CBA — Original Price",      ylab = "Price (AUD)")
plot_ts(log_train, main = "CBA — Log-Transformed Price", ylab = "log(Price)")





CBA price series before and after log transformation. The log transform compresses the multiplicative variance and renders fluctuations more symmetric — a prerequisite for ARIMA modelling.




Code
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
4.3 Normality Check on Log-Transformed Series


Code
plot_norm(log_train,
          qq_title   = "QQ — log(CBA Price)",
          hist_title = "Histogram — log(CBA Price)",
          xlab       = "log(Price)")





Q-Q plot and histogram of the log-transformed CBA price series. The near-linear QQ alignment and symmetric histogram confirm that the log transformation has produced an approximately normal marginal distribution.






Code
# Shapiro-Wilk test on the log-transformed series.
# H0: data are normally distributed.
sw_log <- run_sw(log_train, "Shapiro-Wilk test — log-transformed CBA price.")
sw_log$table



Shapiro-Wilk test — log-transformed CBA price.


Test Statistic (W)
p-value

The Q-Q plot and histogram both confirm that the log transformation has produced a distribution that is far more symmetric and bell-shaped than the raw price levels. The Shapiro-Wilk test result will be assessed alongside the stationarity findings below.

text:
5.1 First Differencing

5.1.1 Visual Inspection


Code
diff_y_train <- diff(y_train, differences = 1)

par(mfrow = c(2, 1), cex = 0.85)
plot_ts(y_train, main = "(a) Original CBA Price", ylab = "Price (AUD)")
plot_ts(diff_y_train, main = "(b) First-Differenced Series (d = 1)",
        ylab = "Δ Price (AUD)", add_mean = TRUE)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))








Figure 8: Original CBA price series (top) and first-differenced series (bottom). The differenced series fluctuates around zero (dashed blue line), confirming the stochastic trend has been successfully removed. Large spikes near 2025.5 correspond to high-impact trading days.







Code
plot_acf_pacf(diff_y_train,
              acf_title  = "ACF — Δ CBA Price (d = 1)",
              pacf_title = "PACF — Δ CBA Price (d = 1)")








Figure 9: ACF (left) and PACF (right) of the first-differenced CBA series. The immediate drop-off with most bars within the 95% confidence bands confirms stationarity and reveals the underlying ARMA structure for model specification.







5.1.2 Statistical Tests

Trend removal (Figure 8). The upward trend in panel (a) is completely removed in panel (b). The differenced series fluctuates around a stable horizontal mean of approximately zero, confirming that first-order differencing was sufficient; a second difference is not required and would risk over-differencing.

These transient volatility clusters are characteristic of bank equity daily returns and reflect high-impact events — RBA cash rate decisions, APRA regulatory announcements, global credit risk repricing. The ARIMA framework models the autocorrelative structure; persistent volatility clustering would require a GARCH extension.

PACF for AR order (p) identification. Significant PACF spikes at lag 5 (and lag 8) suggest potential AR influence at those intervals. An ARIMA(5,1,0) is implied by strict PACF reading, though parsimony and BIC may favour simpler alternatives.

# ADF/PP: H0 = unit root (non-stationary) → reject H0 to confirm stationarity.
# KPSS:   H0 = stationary               → fail to reject H0 to confirm stationarity.
# Convergence across all three tests provides strong evidence for d = 1.
stat_d1 <- check_stationarity(
  diff_log_train,

Interpretation of stationarity tests: A well-functioning ARIMA modelling approach requires convergence of evidence across multiple tests, because each test has its own statistical weaknesses:

ADF and Phillips-Perron tests: Both test H₀ that the series has a unit root. A p-value below 0.05 leads us to reject H₀ and conclude stationarity. For daily financial returns, which exhibit strong mean-reversion by construction, we expect both tests to reject the null decisively. This confirms that one round of differencing on the log series is sufficient — the daily log-returns of CBA do not require further differencing.
KPSS test: The KPSS operates under the opposite null — H₀ is that the series is stationary. A p-value above 0.05 means we fail to reject H₀, corroborating the ADF/PP evidence. This convergence from three independent tests provides robust statistical grounding for the decision that d = 1 (one regular difference applied to the log series) is the correct integration order.

Decision: The log-return series (first difference of log price) is stationary. We proceed with d = 1 applied to the log-transformed series, and all ARIMA model fitting will be conducted with lambda = 0 (log) so that Arima() applies the Box-Cox transformation internally. The integration order is confirmed as d = 1.

ACF and PACF of the first-differenced log(CBA) price series (daily log-returns). After differencing, the slow ACF decay of the raw series has been removed, revealing the underlying ARMA structure for model specification.




The ACF and PACF of the differenced series are the primary model specification tools. Unlike the raw series (where the slow ACF decay dominated everything), the stationary log-return series now reveals the short-memory autocorrelative structure that ARIMA parameters will capture. We interpret these plots in the next section.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
6.1 Reading ACF and PACF
The rules for translating ACF and PACF patterns into AR and MA orders (Cryer & Chan, 2008):

ACF cuts off at lag q, PACF decays geometrically → MA(q) process
PACF cuts off at lag p, ACF decays geometrically → AR(p) process
Both decay geometrically → mixed ARMA(p,q) process

For daily financial returns, it is well-established that the series tend to exhibit only weak short-lag autocorrelation — the majority of the “signal” was in the trend, which we have now removed by differencing.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
6.2 Extended Autocorrelation Function (EACF)
The EACF provides a supplementary order identification tool that is particularly useful when neither the ACF nor the PACF gives a clean cutoff pattern. It displays a triangular pattern of symbols (‘o’ indicates a corner of the triangle — a potential model), guiding us toward candidate (p, q) combinations.


Code
# eacf() from the TSA package.

# for daily financial returns — parsimony favours lower orders.

# of white noise, indicating candidate ARMA(p,q) specifications.

Figure 12: ARIMA(0,1,1) fitted values overlaid on the original CBA price series under CSS (left) and ML (right). Near-identical trajectories indicate a numerically stable, well-identified model.





The MA(1) coefficient θ₁ captures the one-period lingering effect of a price shock — for example, a surprise RBA announcement that initiates a price adjustment which fades within one trading day. For the model to be well-identified, θ₁ must be statistically significant (p < 0.05) under both CSS and ML, and must satisfy the invertibility condition |θ₁| < 1. If the MA(1) term is non-significant, this implies CBA daily price changes carry no exploitable one-day shock memory — consistent with a pure random walk (ARIMA(0,1,0)). As shown in Figure 12, near-identical CSS and ML fitted trajectories confirm numerical stability regardless of coefficient significance.

8.1.3 Interpretation
Residual time series (Figure 19, top-left). The standardised residuals scatter within ±3σ throughout most of the sample with no systematic trend or curvature. The notable spike near 2025.5 corresponds to the level-shift event from the EDA — a high-impact trading day beyond the ARIMA model’s assumption of homogeneous Gaussian innovations. This is a known limitation of linear models for financial data, not a model mis-specification per se.
Histogram (Figure 19, top-right). The residual distribution is broadly symmetric and centred near zero. Any excess kurtosis visible in the tails reflects the well-known fat-tail property of daily equity returns — a feature that a GARCH extension would explicitly model.
QQ plot (Figure 19, bottom-left). The central portion of the QQ plot aligns well with the theoretical line, suggesting approximate normality for typical trading days. Tail deviations correspond to the extreme observations associated with the 2025.5 event.
ACF of residuals (Figure 19, bottom-right). All autocorrelation bars fall within the 95% confidence bands, confirming that any short-lag structure has been absorbed. No systematic pattern remains.
tsdiag panel (Figure 20). The Ljung-Box p-values in the bottom panel remain consistently above 0.05 across all lags — the definitive Module 7 pass criterion.
Formal tests. Ljung-Box p = 0.311 (PASS — white noise). Shapiro-Wilk p = 0 (FAIL — normality violated). Normality failures in daily equity residuals are expected due to fat tails; the Ljung-Box result is the primary adequacy criterion.

CSS–ML divergence. As noted in Figure 14, any divergence between CSS and ML fitted values for the AR₂ or MA₂ parameters is a warning sign. Under ML estimation, the likelihood surface for over-parameterised ARMA models can be nearly flat in the direction of higher-order parameters, leading to numerically unstable estimates and non-significant z-values for AR₂ and MA₂.
Formal tests. Ljung-Box p-value: 0.6693 (PASS). Shapiro-Wilk p-value: 0 (FAIL). An ARIMA(2,1,2) that achieves similar or worse test results than the simpler models, combined with non-significant higher-order parameters, will be eliminated in the model selection stage.

text:
10.2 Final Model Selection and Justification
Selection follows the three-stage Box–Jenkins framework:
Stage 1 — Information Criteria. BIC (with its stronger parsimony penalty of ln(n) per parameter versus AIC’s 2) is the preferred criterion for near-random-walk financial series. Lower-order models that pass residual diagnostics are almost always BIC-optimal for daily equity returns.
Stage 2 — Parameter Stability. Coefficients must be significant under both CSS and ML with consistent estimates across methods. Any model where ML finds a parameter non-significant while CSS finds it significant should be treated with suspicion — CSS’s conditional approximation is less rigorous.
Stage 3 — Residual Adequacy. The model must pass the Ljung-Box white-noise criterion (p > 0.05) and exhibit approximately normal residuals. For daily CBA returns, fat-tail non-normality is expected and does not constitute grounds for rejection.

              pacf_title = "PACF — CBA Raw Price")








Figure 2: ACF (left) and PACF (right) of the raw CBA price series. The ACF’s slow linear decay and the PACF’s single dominant spike at lag 1, confirming non-stationarity before any statistical testing.







3.1.2 Statistical Tests

  "Stationarity Tests — Raw CBA Price (Training Sample)")

3.1.3 Interpretation
Trend. Figure 1 shows a sustained upward quadratic trend from approximately AUD 110 at the start of 2024 to a peak near AUD 185 by mid-2025. The horizontal mean line sits well below the peak of the series, confirming the absence of a stable central tendency — a necessary condition for ARIMA modelling is violated from the outset.
Seasonality. There is no evidence of a fixed-frequency repeating cycle within this two-year window. Price fluctuations appear stochastic rather than deterministic, suggesting that a non-seasonal ARIMA specification is appropriate. Any apparent periodicity aligns with irregular macroeconomic events (RBA meeting cycles, CBA earnings seasons) rather than a strict calendar pattern.
Heteroscedasticity. The magnitude of daily fluctuations increases materially as the price level rises, particularly after 2025.0. This proportional relationship between price level and volatility — a characteristic of geometric Brownian motion — suggests that the raw series will require variance-stabilising investigation before ARIMA modelling.
Change Point. A significant level shift occurs shortly after 2025.5, where the price drops from roughly AUD 175 to AUD 150 in a very short interval — likely reflecting a macro shock such as an unexpected RBA policy decision, changes to bank capital requirements, or a global credit event — followed by elevated volatility and partial recovery.
ACF — slow linear decay (Figure 2, left). The autocorrelations remain large and positive across many lags, declining so slowly that they barely enter the significance bands even at lag 40. This persistence confirms the series has very long memory: each day’s price level carries the weight of all preceding observations forward indefinitely.
PACF — single dominant spike at lag 1 (Figure 2, right). Once the chain of correlations is removed, only the immediately preceding observation retains direct predictive power. This is the canonical PACF signature of a unit-root process.
Stationarity tests. The ADF (p = 0.777) and PP (p = 0.6423) tests both fail to reject the null hypothesis of a unit root, while KPSS (p = 0.01) rejects its stationarity null. All three tests agree: the series contains a stochastic trend that must be removed before modelling.

3.2.3 Interpretation
Central tendency. The mean (AUD 149.11) and median (AUD 151.2) are closely aligned, but this proximity is misleading: it results from the series spending roughly equal time in its lower and upper price ranges, not from any genuine normality.
Dispersion. The standard deviation of AUD 18.72 reflects the wide price range traversed over the training window — a direct consequence of the sustained upward trend in Figure 1 rather than a meaningful measure of single-period volatility.
QQ plot (Figure 3, left). The S-shaped deviation from the theoretical line at both tails indicates lighter tails than a normal distribution — a natural consequence of the bounded lower tail (prices cannot fall below zero) and the right-skew imposed by the trend.
Histogram (Figure 3, right). The multimodal profile with at least two visible clusters corresponds to the lower pre-2025 and higher 2025-peak price ranges. No single normal density can describe this bimodal structure, which is a direct symptom of the non-stationarity identified above.
Shapiro-Wilk test. With W = 0.9588 and p ≈ 2e-09, we decisively reject normality. Transformation and differencing are required before valid ARIMA estimation.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
3.3 Synthesis of EDA Findings
Variance stabilisation required. The Shapiro-Wilk test (p ≈ 0), S-shaped QQ deviations, and visually apparent heteroscedasticity in Figure 1 collectively confirm non-constant variance proportional to price level. A Box-Cox transformation will be evaluated in Section 4.
Mean stabilisation required. The unanimous agreement of ADF (p = 0.777), PP (p = 0.6423), and KPSS (p = 0.01) confirms the presence of a unit root. The slow ACF decay in Figure 2 provides visual corroboration. First-order differencing (d = 1) will be applied in Section 5.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
4.3 Decision: Proceed Without Transformation
The Box-Cox transformation produces no substantive improvement in normality, variance stability, or stationarity. In accordance with the principle of parsimony (Cryer & Chan, 2008), the transformation is rejected. The analysis proceeds on the original adjusted price series. The next phase applies first-order differencing (d = 1) to stabilise the mean.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
section: 6.1 Order Identification from ACF and PACF
text:
6.1 Order Identification from ACF and PACF
The standard rules (Cryer & Chan, 2008) for translating Figure 9 into ARMA orders are:

ACF cuts off at lag q, PACF decays geometrically → MA(q)
PACF cuts off at lag p, ACF decays geometrically → AR(p)
Both decay geometrically → mixed ARMA(p,q)

Following these rules, the first-differenced series exhibits predominantly white-noise behaviour in early lags (1–4 and 6–11), with no persistent autocorrelation structure. Isolated spikes at lag 5 and lag 8 are treated as potential signals but evaluated against lower-order models through EACF and BIC — attempting to model lag 5 or lag 8 directly through standard AR/MA orders risks over-parameterisation. The analysis prioritises parsimonious low-order candidates consistent with the near-random-walk nature of daily equity returns.

The ‘o’ symbols mark the upper-left corner of a white-noise triangle, identifying candidate ARMA(p,q) pairs. The EACF vertex at AR = 0, MA = 1 identifies ARIMA(0,1,1) as the most parsimonious candidate; a secondary ‘o’ at (1,1) suggests ARIMA(1,1,1) as a viable mixed alternative. The EACF effectively ignores the isolated high-lag spikes from Figure 9, reinforcing the parsimony argument.

The lowest-BIC row identifies ARIMA(7,1,0) — where only the 7th-order AR component minimises information loss — potentially capturing a weekly cyclicality or delayed market reaction specific to CBA. The second-ranked row suggests a more complex higher-order specification; the substantial BIC penalty increase from row 1 to row 2 signals diminishing returns from additional parameters.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
8.8 Synthesis of Residual Evidence
Across all seven candidates, three evaluation questions drive the final selection:

Does any model leave significant ACF spikes in the residuals, indicating unmodelled autocorrelative structure?
Do the tsdiag Ljung-Box p-values remain consistently above 0.05 across all lags?
Does added complexity yield any visible improvement in residual behaviour relative to simpler models?

For daily CBA price changes — near a random walk — over-parameterisation is the dominant risk. Any model that passes the Ljung-Box criterion at a lower parameter count than a higher-order alternative is to be preferred. Residual normality violations are expected due to the fat-tail nature of daily equity returns and do not constitute grounds for model rejection provided the Ljung-Box criterion is satisfied.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
10.1 Information Criteria and Accuracy


Code
all_models_ml <- list(

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
11.4 Forecast Interpretation
Convergence to a flat trajectory (Figure 20). The ARIMA(0,1,1) point forecast quickly converges to a near-flat line after the first few periods. This is a fundamental property of the integrated model: the MA(1) term provides a small correction for the most recent shock, but beyond the first forecast period, the conditional expectation of future returns is approximately zero, and the expected price path follows the long-run drift. For an MA(1) model, the forecast horizon beyond which the model provides no additional predictive power relative to a random walk is exactly one period — the lag-1 memory of the MA term.
Widening prediction intervals. The prediction intervals in Figure 20 widen symmetrically as the forecast horizon extends, reflecting the accumulation of uncertainty in the integrated (I(1)) process. For a pure random walk, the forecast variance grows linearly with horizon (σ² × h). The ARIMA(0,1,1) widening is marginally slower due to the MA correction, but converges to the random walk behaviour after one step. The 95% PI should contain approximately 95% of the 30 actual test-set observations; if it does not, the model has under-estimated the test-period volatility — possible if the test period coincides with a market shock of the type seen at the 2025.5 event.
Practical implications for CBA monitoring. For risk managers and portfolio analysts tracking CBA positions, these widening intervals provide a principled, model-based quantification of the price range within which CBA is expected to trade over the next six calendar weeks. The ARIMA framework is transparent about the fundamental limit of price predictability for large-cap equities: beyond the single-period MA correction, the best forecast of tomorrow’s CBA price is today’s price plus a small drift — a direct empirical manifestation of market efficiency.

2 Why This Project?
This project was born out of my strong interest in finance, statsitics, time series analysis forecasting and modelling. It started with my professors, gained momentum from a book, and came to life through a workshop.
Dr Haydar Demirhan at RMIT University, whose teaching and encouragement pushed me to tackle a real-world forecasting project. He taught me something I’ll never forget: AI can generate statistical output, but it cannot replicate human judgment. It cannot interpret evidence, weigh competing models, or make the informed decisions that drive genuine analysis forward.
Around the same time, a former manager handed me a book that would become my practical bible Forecasting: Principles and Practice (3rd ed.) by Rob J Hyndman and George Athanasopoulos (FPP3), practical guide to time series forecasting.
Then came the moment that truly opened my eyes. I attended a workshop organised by Professor Hyndman’s PhD student Mitchell O’Hara-Wild’s “Forecasting with fable” at WOMBAT 2025 (workshop link). That session opened my eyes to the power of modern forecasting tools in R, especially the fable ecosystem.
This project is the intersection of everything I’ve learned: statistical rigour, practical tools, and the human judgment in this process.

section: 5.2 Decision: Proceed With First Difference Original Series
text:
5.2 Decision: Proceed With First Difference Original Series
Based on the evidence above, we will proceed with the original series (non-transformed) at d=1. In time-series modeling, we adhere to the Principle of Parsimony. Further differencing (e.g., d=2) is rejected for the following reasons:

Risk of Over-differencing: Since d=1 has already achieved a stable mean and statistically significant stationarity across ADF, PP, and KPSS tests, any further differencing would unnecessarily increase the variance of the residuals and introduce artificial dependencies (moving average artifacts) into the model.
Loss of Information: Each level of differencing removes information. d=1 effectively transforms “Price” into “Daily Change” (Returns). A second difference (d=2) would model the “change in the change” (Acceleration), which is often too noisy for financial forecasting and makes the final results significantly harder to interpret in a business context.
Parsimonious Specification: A stationary d=1 series allows for a simpler ARIMA structure that is less likely to overfit the training data, ensuring better generalization on the 30-day hold-out test set.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
section: 5.2 Decision: Proceed With First-Differenced Original Series
text:
5.2 Decision: Proceed With First-Differenced Original Series
Based on the evidence above, the analysis proceeds with the original (untransformed) series at d = 1. Further differencing (d = 2) is rejected for three reasons:

Risk of over-differencing. d = 1 has already achieved statistically confirmed stationarity across all three tests. A second difference introduces artificial moving-average structure and inflates residual variance unnecessarily.
Loss of interpretability. d = 1 transforms prices into daily changes (returns) — a natural, meaningful financial quantity. d = 2 would model the “change in changes” (acceleration), which has no natural financial interpretation and is highly sensitive to noise.
Parsimony. A stationary d = 1 series allows for a simpler ARIMA structure less likely to overfit, preserving out-of-sample generalisation on the 90-day test set.

Residual ACF (Figure 29, bottom-right). The critical assessment is whether the lag-5 and lag-8 spikes from Figure 9 have been absorbed. If ARIMA(7,1,0) resolves these spikes while ARIMA(3,1,0) does not, the higher-order model is earning its BIC cost.
Formal tests. Ljung-Box p = 0.6806 (PASS). Shapiro-Wilk p = 0 (FAIL).

8.7.3 Interpretation
Over-parameterisation signal (?@fig-resid-panel-917). For daily CBA price changes — close to a random walk — a model with 16 parameters is almost certainly over-specified. If the visual diagnostics show no improvement over Figure 29 or Figure 25, all the additional parameters are absorbing in-sample noise at the cost of out-of-sample forecast accuracy. The histogram and QQ plot in particular should be compared: if they show no improvement in residual normality, the model’s additional complexity offers nothing beyond what simpler models already achieve.
Numerical stability. The ML likelihood surface for a high-order ARMA model is often nearly flat in the direction of higher-order parameters, producing slow convergence, numerically sensitive estimates, and large standard errors. Any convergence warnings from Arima() are direct evidence of this instability.
Formal tests. Ljung-Box p = 0.9583 (PASS). Shapiro-Wilk p = 0 (FAIL). If this model achieves similar or worse Ljung-Box results than lower-order candidates, it is eliminated from consideration regardless of its BIC ranking.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
11.1 ARIMA Forecast (Selected Model)


Code
# fc_arima already defined above as: forecast(best_model, h = h)
# No lambda needed — model was fit on raw prices
plot_forecast_simple(fc_arima, y_train, y_test, best_model_name)








Figure 33: Selected ARIMA(0,1,1) 90-day forecast. The point forecast (dark line) converges rapidly to a flat trajectory — the fundamental property of an MA(1) integrated model. Shaded regions show 80% and 95% prediction intervals; red line shows actual test-set prices.





Convergence to flat trajectory (Figure 33). The MA(1) integrated model provides a one-period correction for the most recent shock; beyond that single period, the expected future price change is zero and the forecast collapses to a flat level. This is an honest reflection of the near-random-walk nature of CBA prices rather than a model failure — it correctly communicates that the historical autocorrelative structure does not support directional price prediction beyond one step.
Widening intervals. Prediction intervals widen symmetrically as the forecast horizon extends, reflecting the linear accumulation of uncertainty in an I(1) process. The 95% PI should contain approximately 95% of the 90 test-set observations; coverage below this threshold indicates the model has under-estimated test-period volatility, likely because the test window contains a structural event similar to the 2025.5 shock.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
11.2 Benchmark Forecasts


Code
fc_bench <- benchmark_forecasts(y_train, y_test)




Code
plot_forecast_simple(fc_bench$Naive, y_train, y_test, "Naive")








Figure 34: Naive forecast: the last observed training price carried forward as a flat line. This represents the simplest possible forecast and serves as the baseline every model must outperform.







Code
plot_forecast_simple(fc_bench$Drift, y_train, y_test, "Drift")








Figure 35: Drift forecast: linear extrapolation of the historical average daily change. The drift captures the long-run upward trend in CBA prices but ignores any mean-reversion or shock dynamics.







Code
plot_forecast_simple(fc_bench$Mean, y_train, y_test, "Mean")








Figure 36: Mean forecast: the historical training average projected as a constant. This assumes CBA prices will immediately revert to the long-run mean — an assumption violated by the unit-root evidence.





The three benchmark methods — Naive, Drift, and Mean — represent simple but important baselines. The Naive method (last observation carried forward) is theoretically equivalent to a random walk forecast and should perform competitively with ARIMA models on near-random-walk data. The Drift method extrapolates the historical average daily change, capturing the training-period upward trend. The Mean method projects the historical average and will perform poorly whenever the test-period price level deviates materially from the training mean.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
11.3 Exponential Smoothing (ETS)


Code
fc_ets_result <- ets_forecast(y_train, h = h)


Warning in ets(y_train): I can't handle data with frequency greater than 24.
Seasonality will be ignored. Try stlf() if you need seasonal forecasts.


Code
cat("ETS model selected:", fc_ets_result$model$method, "\n")


ETS model selected: ETS(M,N,N) 




Code
plot_forecast_simple(fc_ets_result$forecast, y_train, y_test, 
                     model_name = paste("ETS(", fc_ets_result$model$method, ")"))








Figure 37: ETS forecast with automatically selected error, trend, and seasonal components. ETS adapts to the local level and trend in the training data through exponentially declining weights on past observations.





Exponential Smoothing State Space (ETS) models assign exponentially declining weights to past observations, with the rate of decay governed by smoothing parameters. For a trending financial series like CBA, ETS typically selects an additive trend component (model type A,A,N), allowing it to project the local upward trend forward. The ets() function’s automatic selection process chooses the component structure that minimises the training-set information criterion, providing a data-driven alternative to the manual ARIMA specification.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks

11.4 Automatic ARIMA


Code
fc_auto_result <- auto_arima_forecast(y_train, h = h)
cat("auto.arima selected:", as.character(fc_auto_result$model), "\n")


auto.arima selected: ARIMA(0,1,0) 




Code
plot_forecast_simple(fc_auto_result$forecast, y_train, y_test,
                     model_name = paste("auto.arima:", 
                                        as.character(fc_auto_result$model)))








Figure 38: auto.arima() forecast using an exhaustive (non-stepwise, exact likelihood) search over ARIMA order combinations. This serves as an algorithmic benchmark against the manually specified ARIMA(0,1,1).





auto.arima() with stepwise = FALSE and approximation = FALSE performs an exhaustive search over ARIMA order combinations using exact likelihood. Comparing its selected order against the manually specified ARIMA(0,1,1) validates the model specification process: if auto.arima() selects the same model, the manual analysis is confirmed. If it selects a different specification, the discrepancy warrants investigation — either the manual specification missed structure, or auto.arima() over-fits to the training sample.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
11.5 TBATS


Code
fc_tbats_result <- tbats_forecast(y_train, h = h)
cat("TBATS model fitted successfully.\n")


TBATS model fitted successfully.




Code
plot_forecast_simple(fc_tbats_result$forecast, y_train, y_test, "TBATS")








Figure 39: TBATS forecast — handles complex seasonal patterns including multiple seasonal periods. For daily financial data with frequency = 252, TBATS may capture annual trading cycles that ARIMA cannot model.





TBATS (Trigonometric, Box-Cox, ARMA errors, Trend, Seasonal) decomposes the series into trend, seasonal, and error components, handling multiple seasonal periods and non-integer frequencies. For daily CBA data with frequency = 252, TBATS can model any annual trading cycle (e.g., year-end institutional rebalancing) that ARIMA treats as noise. If TBATS achieves materially better test-set accuracy than ARIMA, this suggests a seasonal component with practical significance for CBA price dynamics.

11.6 Forecast Comparison


Code
all_forecasts <- list(
  "ARIMA(0,1,1)" = fc_arima,
  "Naive"        = fc_bench$Naive,
  "Drift"        = fc_bench$Drift,
  "Mean"         = fc_bench$Mean,
  "ETS"          = fc_ets_result$forecast,
  "auto.arima"   = fc_auto_result$forecast,
  "TBATS"        = fc_tbats_result$forecast
)

plot_forecast_comparison(all_forecasts, y_train, y_test,
                         title = "CBA.AX — All Forecast Methods vs Actual (90-Day Test Set)")








Figure 40: All forecasting methods overlaid for direct visual comparison. The red dashed line shows actual test-set prices. Methods that track the actual series most closely in direction and magnitude achieve the best out-of-sample accuracy.





Figure 40 provides the definitive visual comparison of all methods against the actual test-set prices. Methods that diverge substantially from the actual series — particularly any that project the training trend steeply upward while actual prices are flat or declining — are demonstrating the over-reliance on historical momentum that makes directional forecasting of near-random-walk equity prices so difficult. Methods that remain close to the last observed price (Naive, ARIMA) are implicitly acknowledging the random-walk structure and will typically outperform more opinionated methods on error metrics.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
This study applies a systematic ARIMA time series modelling workflow to the weekly adjusted closing price of Commonwealth Bank of Australia (CBA.AX) — one of Australia’s “Big Four” banks and the largest company by market capitalisation on the ASX 200. CBA’s price movements are closely watched as a barometer of the broader Australian financial sector, domestic housing market conditions, and the Reserve Bank of Australia’s (RBA) monetary policy trajectory.
The analysis follows:

Exploratory Data Analysis — visual inspection of trend, variance, and distributional properties.
Transformation — Box-Cox variance stabilisation where warranted.
Differencing & Stationarity — achieving a time-invariant mean via differencing, verified by formal unit-root tests.
Model Specification — AR and MA order identification via ACF, PACF, EACF, and BIC tables.
Model Fitting & Residual Diagnostics — CSS and ML estimation with full residual analysis and overparameterised model checks.
Forecasting — producing point forecasts and prediction intervals using multiple methods.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
3 Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

3.1 Stationarity Inspection

3.1.1 Visual Inspection


Code
plot_ts(y_train, main = "CBA Adjusted Closing Price — Training Sample",
        ylab = "Price (AUD)", add_mean = TRUE)








Figure 1: CBA adjusted closing price — training sample. The plot visualises the stock’s historical performance from late 2021 through early 2026.







Code
plot_acf_pacf(y_train,
              acf_title  = "ACF — CBA Raw Price",
              pacf_title = "PACF — CBA Raw Price")








Figure 2: ACF (left) and PACF (right) of the raw CBA price series. The ACF’s slow linear decay and the PACF’s single dominant spike at lag 1 confirm non-stationarity before any statistical testing.







3.1.2 Statistical Tests


Code
stat_raw <- check_adf(y_train,
  "Stationarity Test — Raw CBA Price (Training Sample)")
stat_raw$table



Stationarity Test — Raw CBA Price (Training Sample)


Test Statistic
p-value




-1.7883
6.6e-01







3.1.3 Interpretation
Trend. Figure 1 shows a sustained upward quadratic trend from approximately AUD 75.52 at the start to a peak near AUD 180.13 by mid-2025. This sustained growth indicates a non-stationary mean, confirming that the series requires mean stabilisation before modelling.
Seasonality. There is no evidence of a fixed-frequency repeating cycle within this observed window. Price fluctuations appear stochastic rather than deterministic. Any apparent periodicity aligns with irregular macroeconomic events rather than a strict calendar pattern. This suggests that a non-seasonal ARIMA specification is appropriate, and no seasonal differencing or seasonal parameters are required.
Heteroscedasticity. The variance appears relatively stable (homoscedastic) throughout the period. The magnitude of fluctuations does not increase proportionally with the mean, suggesting the series may not require a variance-stabilising transformation.
Behavior. The series demonstrates strong autoregressive (AR) behavior, where each observation is highly dependent on its previous value. The sustained climb suggests that current resistance levels serve as a strong predictor for the following period.
Change point. No change point is observed.






Note



A change point in time series analysis refers to a transition where the underlying statistical properties of the data, such as the mean, variance, or trend, undergo a sudden and persistent shift.


ACF — slow linear decay (Figure 2, left). The autocorrelations remain large and positive across many lags, declining so slowly that they barely enter the significance bands even at lag 60. This persistence confirms the series has very long memory: each week’s adjusted price level carries the weight of all preceding observations forward indefinitely.
PACF — single dominant spike at lag 1 (Figure 2, right). Once the chain of correlations is removed, only the immediately preceding observation retains direct predictive power. This is the canonical PACF signature of a unit-root process.
Stationarity tests. The series contains a stochastic trend. The ADF (p = 0.6649) fails to reject the unit-root null, confirming the non-stationarity that must be removed before modelling.



3.2 Normality & Distributional Inspection

3.2.1 Visual Inspection


Code
plot_norm(y_train, qq_title  = "QQ Plot — Raw CBA Price",
          hist_title = "Histogram — Raw CBA Price", xlab = "Price (AUD)")








Figure 3: QQ plot (left) and histogram (right) for the raw CBA training price series. The S-shaped deviation from the reference line and the non-symmetric histogram reject the assumption that the data follow a normal distribution.







3.2.2 Statistical Tests


Code
Summary <- c(
  Mean   = round(mean(y_train),               2),
  Median = round(median(y_train),             2),
  SD     = round(sd(y_train),                 2),
  Min    = round(min(y_train),                2),
  Max    = round(max(y_train),                2),
  Range  = round(max(y_train) - min(y_train), 2)
)
knitr::kable(t(as.data.frame(Summary)),
  caption = "Descriptive statistics — CBA.AX adjusted closing price (training sample).",
  align   = "c")



Descriptive statistics — CBA.AX adjusted closing price (training sample).



Mean
Median
SD
Min
Max
Range




Summary
113.03

75.52
180.13

Code
normality_raw <- check_normality(y_train, "Shapiro-Wilk Test — Raw CBA Price")
normality_raw$table



Shapiro-Wilk Test — Raw CBA Price


Test Statistic (W)
p-value




0.8363
1.5e-15







3.2.3 Interpretation
Central tendency. The mean (AUD 113.03) and median (AUD 94.94) are closely aligned, but this proximity is misleading: it results from the series spending roughly equal time in its lower and upper price ranges, not from any genuine normality.
Dispersion. The standard deviation of AUD 31.44 reflects the wide price range traversed over the training window — a direct consequence of the sustained upward trend in Figure 1 rather than a meaningful measure of single-period volatility.
QQ plot (Figure 3, left). The S-shaped deviation from the theoretical line at both tails indicates lighter tails than a normal distribution — a natural consequence of the bounded lower tail (prices cannot fall below zero) and the right-skew imposed by the trend.
Histogram (Figure 3, right). The multimodal profile with at least two visible clusters corresponds to the lower pre-2025 and higher 2025-peak price ranges. No single normal density can describe this bimodal structure, which is a direct symptom of the non-stationarity identified above.
Shapiro-Wilk test. With W = 0.8363 and p ≈ 1.5e-15, we decisively reject normality. Transformation and differencing are required before valid ARIMA estimation.



3.3 Synthesis of EDA Findings
Variance stabilisation required. The Shapiro-Wilk test (p ≈ 0), S-shaped QQ deviations, and visually apparent heteroscedasticity in Figure 1 collectively confirm non-constant variance proportional to price level. A Box-Cox transformation will be evaluated in the next section.
Mean stabilisation required. The ADF (p = 0.6649) confirms the presence of a unit root. The slow ACF decay in Figure 2 provides visual corroboration. First-order differencing (d = 1) will be applied to address this.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
4 Transformation

4.1 Box-Cox Analysis

4.1.1 Visual Inspection


Code
BC <- BoxCox.ar(y = y_train, lambda = seq(-2, 2, 0.01))


Warning in arima0(x, order = c(i, 0L, 0L), include.mean = demean): possible
convergence problem: optim gave code = 1








Figure 4: The Box-Cox log-likelihood profile for the CBA training series. The peak of the profile indicates the optimal λ, and the dashed lines bound the 95% confidence interval.







4.1.2 Statistical Tests


Code
lambda_opt   <- BC$lambda[which.max(BC$loglike)]
lambda_lower <- BC$ci[1]
lambda_upper <- BC$ci[2]

bc_df <- data.frame(
  Parameter = c("Optimal λ", "95% CI Lower", "95% CI Upper",
                "CI includes λ = 0 (log)?", "CI includes λ = 1 (none)?"),
  Value     = c(round(lambda_opt,   4),
                round(lambda_lower, 4),
                round(lambda_upper, 4),
                ifelse(lambda_lower <= 0 & lambda_upper >= 0, "YES", "NO"),
                ifelse(lambda_lower <= 1 & lambda_upper >= 1, "YES", "NO"))
)
knitr::kable(bc_df, caption = "Box-Cox parameter estimation results.",
             align = "lc", col.names = c("Parameter", "Value"))



Box-Cox parameter estimation results.


Parameter
Value




Optimal λ
-0.3


95% CI Lower
-0.63


95% CI Upper
0.02


CI includes λ = 0 (log)?
YES


CI includes λ = 1 (none)?
NO







4.1.3 Interpretation
The optimal λ (-0.3) sits close to zero, and the 95% CI from Figure 4 determines whether a log transformation is statistically supported. For CBA weekly prices, the CI typically includes λ = 0 and excludes λ = 1 — providing formal support for the log transform and aligning with financial econometric theory: log-prices follow a random walk with drift, converting multiplicative price movements into additive log-returns.



4.2 Applied Transformation

4.2.1 Visual Inspection


Code
BC_train <- (y_train^lambda_opt - 1) / lambda_opt
BC_test  <- (y_test^lambda_opt  - 1) / lambda_opt

par(mfrow = c(2, 1))
plot_ts(y_train,  main = "CBA — Original Series",            ylab = "Price (AUD)")
plot_ts(BC_train, main = "CBA — Box-Cox Transformed Series", ylab = "BC(Price)")
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))








Figure 5: Original CBA price series (top) and Box-Cox transformed series (bottom). A successful transformation would show reduced heteroscedasticity — specifically, fluctuation amplitude becoming more uniform across the sample.







Code
plot_acf_pacf(BC_train,
              acf_title  = "ACF — Box-Cox Transformed Series",
              pacf_title = "PACF — Box-Cox Transformed Series")








Figure 6: ACF (left) and PACF (right) of the Box-Cox transformed series. The slow ACF decay and single dominant PACF spike at lag 1 mirror the patterns in Figure 2, confirming the transformation has not altered the fundamental non-stationary structure.







Code
plot_norm(BC_train, qq_title   = "QQ Plot — BC Transformed Series",
          hist_title = "Histogram — BC Transformed Series", xlab = "BC(Price)")








Figure 7: QQ plot (left) and histogram (right) for the Box-Cox transformed series. Near-identical tail behaviour to the original series (Figure 3) indicates that the transformation has not materially improved distributional normality.







4.2.2 Statistical Tests


Code
normality_bc <- check_normality(BC_train,
  "Shapiro-Wilk Test — Box-Cox Transformed Series")
normality_bc$table



Shapiro-Wilk Test — Box-Cox Transformed Series


Test Statistic (W)
p-value




0.868
7.5e-14







Code
stat_bc <- check_adf(BC_train, "Stationarity Test — Box-Cox Transformed Series")
stat_bc$table



Stationarity Test — Box-Cox Transformed Series


Test Statistic
p-value




-1.9761
5.9e-01







4.2.3 Interpretation
Time series plot (Figure 5). The Box-Cox transformed series retains the same overall shape — the upward trend persists and the amplitude of fluctuations continues to grow after 2025. The transformation has not materially altered the non-stationary structure or visibly reduced heteroscedasticity, consistent with financial series where variance is driven primarily by the stochastic trend.
ACF/PACF (Figure 6). The slow linear ACF decay and single dominant PACF spike at lag 1 are nearly identical to the raw series in Figure 2. Non-stationarity in the mean remains unresolved.
QQ plot and histogram (Figure 7). The S-shaped QQ deviation and multimodal histogram are essentially indistinguishable from Figure 3. No meaningful distributional improvement has been achieved.
Normality. W = 0.868, p = 7.5e-14 — marginally worse than the original series. The normality null is again decisively rejected.
Stationarity. ADF (p = 0.5859) confirms the transformed series remains non-stationary.



4.3 Decision: Proceed Without Transformation
The table below summarises the key test statistics before and after transformation, confirming that the Box-Cox transformation offers no material improvement on either dimension.


Code
bc_compare_df <- data.frame(
  Series        = c("Original", "Box-Cox Transformed"),
  "SW Statistic (W)" = c(round(normality_raw$result$statistic, 4),
                          round(normality_bc$result$statistic,  4)),
  "SW p-value"       = c(format(normality_raw$result$p.value, scientific = TRUE, digits = 2),
                          format(normality_bc$result$p.value,  scientific = TRUE, digits = 2)),
  "ADF Statistic"    = c(round(as.numeric(stat_raw$result$statistic), 4),
                          round(as.numeric(stat_bc$result$statistic),  4)),
  "ADF p-value"      = c(format(stat_raw$result$p.value, scientific = TRUE, digits = 2),
                          format(stat_bc$result$p.value, scientific = TRUE, digits = 2)),
  check.names = FALSE
)
knitr::kable(bc_compare_df,
  caption = "Comparison of normality and stationarity — original vs Box-Cox transformed series.",
  align   = "lcccc")



Comparison of normality and stationarity — original vs Box-Cox transformed series.

The Box-Cox transformation produces no substantive improvement in normality and negligible improvement in variance stability or stationarity. In accordance with the principle of parsimony (Cryer & Chan, 2008), the transformation is rejected. The analysis proceeds on the original adjusted price series. The next phase applies first-order differencing (d = 1) to stabilise the mean.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
5 Addressing Non-Stationarity

5.1 First Differencing

5.1.1 Visual Inspection


Code
diff_y_train <- diff(y_train, differences = 1)

par(mfrow = c(2, 1))
plot_ts(y_train,      main = "(a) Original CBA Price",              ylab = "Price (AUD)")
plot_ts(diff_y_train, main = "(b) First-Differenced Series (d = 1)", ylab = "Δ Price (AUD)",
        add_mean = TRUE)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))








Figure 8: Original CBA price series (top) and first-differenced series (bottom). The differenced series fluctuates around zero, confirming the stochastic trend has been successfully removed.







Code
plot_acf_pacf(diff_y_train,

5.1.2 Statistical Tests

stat_d1 <- check_stationarity(diff_y_train,
  "Stationarity Tests — First-Differenced Series (d = 1)")

5.1.3 Interpretation
Trend removal (Figure 8). The upward trend in the original series (a) is completely removed in the first-differenced series (b). The differenced series fluctuates around a stable horizontal mean of approximately zero, confirming that first-order differencing was sufficient; a second difference is not required and would risk over-differencing.
Volatility and outliers. Figure 8 highlights elevated volatility near 2026. While the ARIMA framework models the linear autocorrelative structure, the presence of volatility clustering suggests that a GARCH extension may be necessary to model the conditional variance if ARCH effects are detected in the residuals.
ACF & PACF (Figure 9). Visual inspection confirms that first-order differencing (d = 1) has successfully achieved stationarity. Both plots display an immediate collapse with the majority of bars within the 95% confidence bounds. However, significant spikes at early lags suggest a residual short-term autocorrelative structure. These spikes will guide the selection of the AR (p) and MA (q) orders during model specification.
Stationarity tests. ADF (p = 0.01) and PP (p = 0.01) both reject the unit-root null; KPSS (p = 0.1) fails to reject its stationarity null. All three tests unanimously confirm stationarity. The integration order is confirmed as d = 1.



5.2 Decision: Proceed With First-Differenced Original Series
Based on the evidence above, the analysis proceeds with the original (untransformed) series at d = 1. Further differencing (d = 2) is rejected for three reasons:
Risk of over-differencing. d = 1 has already achieved statistically confirmed stationarity across all three tests. A second difference introduces artificial moving-average structure and inflates residual variance unnecessarily.
Loss of interpretability. d = 1 transforms prices into weekly changes (returns) — a natural, meaningful financial quantity. d = 2 would model the “change in changes” (acceleration), which has no natural financial interpretation and is highly sensitive to noise.
Parsimony. A stationary d = 1 series allows for a simpler ARIMA structure less likely to overfit, preserving out-of-sample generalisation on the 12-week test set.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
6 Model Specification

6.1 Order Identification from ACF and PACF
Visual inspection of the ACF and PACF plots (Figure 9) of the first-differenced series confirms stationarity, as evidenced by the rapid decay of autocorrelation coefficients toward zero. Despite this stability, isolated spikes suggest a residual autocorrelative signal. To investigate whether these early-lag dependencies are structural components or merely stochastic noise, we applied standard rules (Cryer & Chan, 2008) for translating ACF and PACF plots into ARMA orders:

ACF cuts off at lag q, PACF decays geometrically → MA(q)
PACF cuts off at lag p, ACF decays geometrically → AR(p)
Both decay geometrically → mixed ARMA(p, q)

From Figure 9, the patterns suggest candidates including ARIMA(4,1,2), ARIMA(4,1,3), and ARIMA(3,1,3).

The EACF table identifies the upper-left vertex of the ‘o’ triangle, which represents candidates that effectively reduce the series to white noise. The vertex at AR = 0, MA = 6 identifies ARIMA(0,1,6) as the most parsimonious candidate, while a secondary ‘o’ at (1,6) suggests ARIMA(1,1,6) as a viable mixed alternative. ARIMA(0,1,7) is also included to ensure robust coverage.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
7 Model Fitting
All models are fitted to the raw (untransformed) original price series using Arima(). Both CSS and ML estimation are applied. CSS provides a computationally efficient approximation; ML maximises the exact likelihood. In a sample of this size (n ≈ 250), the two methods should converge to nearly identical estimates — any material divergence is a signal of mis-specification or near-boundary parameters. include.mean = FALSE constrains the drift to zero, consistent with the no-drift assumption for CBA weekly price changes.


Code
# ── EACF Vertex Models ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
model_016_css <- Arima(y_train, order = c(0,1,6), method = "CSS")
model_016_ml  <- Arima(y_train, order = c(0,1,6), method = "ML")

model_116_css <- Arima(y_train, order = c(1,1,6), method = "CSS")

Figure 13





Coefficient interpretation. The ARIMA(0,1,4) models estimated via Conditional Sum of Squares (CSS) and Maximum Likelihood (ML) yield nearly identical, robust parameter estimates. In both models, only the fourth moving average coefficient (ma4) is statistically significant (CSS:β=−0.166,p=0.010; ML:β=−0.164,p=0.011), while the intermediate lags (ma1 through ma3) fail to reject the null hypothesis of being equal to zero (p>0.05).
Residual interpretation. Residual diagnostics confirm that the model successfully captures the data’s underlying dependence structure: the Autocorrelation Function (ACF) spikes remain entirely within the significance bounds, and the Ljung-Box test p-values sit safely above the 0.05 threshold across all 40 lags, confirming that the residuals behave as independent white noise. Although a solitary extreme outlier at the beginning of 2026 introduces slight leptokurtosis into the otherwise normally distributed QQ-plot and histogram, the model is statistically valid for forecasting.
Decision. However, because three out of four parameters are statistically insignificant, the model is overparameterized; a more parsimonious seasonal model should be evaluated against this baseline using Information Criteria (AIC/BIC) and Accuracy Metrics to optimize model efficiency.

7.3 In-Sample Accuracy


Code
# Compute in-sample accuracy for all ML models
acc_list <- lapply(all_models_ml, function(m) accuracy(m)[1, 1:7])
acc_df   <- as.data.frame(do.call(rbind, acc_list))
colnames(acc_df) <- c("ME", "RMSE", "MAE", "MPE", "MAPE", "MASE", "ACF1")
rownames(acc_df) <- names(all_models_ml)

# Composite rank (lower is better on all four metrics)
acc_df$RMSE_Rank <- rank(acc_df$RMSE)
acc_df$MAE_Rank  <- rank(acc_df$MAE)
acc_df$MAPE_Rank <- rank(acc_df$MAPE)
acc_df$MASE_Rank <- rank(acc_df$MASE)
acc_df$Total_Rank <- rowSums(acc_df[, c("RMSE_Rank","MAE_Rank","MAPE_Rank","MASE_Rank")])
acc_df_sorted <- acc_df[order(acc_df$Total_Rank), ]

knitr::kable(
  round(acc_df_sorted[, c("ME","RMSE","MAE","MPE","MAPE","MASE","ACF1")], 4),
  caption = "In-sample accuracy metrics for all candidate models (ML estimation), sorted by composite rank.",

)

We evaluate the selected ARIMA model alongside a comprehensive suite of alternative forecasting methods — benchmarks, exponential smoothing, automatic ARIMA, and TBATS — to provide a complete picture of forecast performance on the 12-week test set. All methods are applied to the original (untransformed) price series.

Code
plot_forecast_simple(fc_arima, y_train, y_test, best_model_name)








Figure 38: ARIMA(3,1,3) CSS-ML — 12-week forecast. Shaded regions show 80% and 95% prediction intervals; red line shows actual test-set prices.





Convergence to flat trajectory (Figure 38). The ARIMA(3,1,3) model provides short-horizon corrections attributable to the AR(3) and MA(3) components; beyond three to four periods, the expected future price change approaches zero and the forecast converges to a flat level. This is an honest reflection of the near-random-walk nature of CBA prices rather than a model failure — it correctly communicates that the historical autocorrelative structure does not support directional price prediction at extended horizons.
Widening intervals. Prediction intervals widen symmetrically as the forecast horizon extends, reflecting the linear accumulation of uncertainty in an I(1) process. Given the residual non-normality identified above, the nominal 95% coverage may understate the true uncertainty around extreme shock events.


11.2 Benchmark Forecasts


Code
fc_bench <- benchmark_forecasts(y_train, y_test)




Code
plot_forecast_simple(fc_bench$Naive, y_train, y_test, "Naive")








Figure 39: Naive forecast: the last observed training price carried forward as a flat line. This represents the simplest possible forecast and serves as the baseline every model must outperform.







Code
plot_forecast_simple(fc_bench$Drift, y_train, y_test, "Drift")








Figure 40: Drift forecast: linear extrapolation of the historical average weekly change. The drift captures the long-run upward trend in CBA prices but ignores any mean-reversion or shock dynamics.







Code
plot_forecast_simple(fc_bench$Mean, y_train, y_test, "Mean")








Figure 41: Mean forecast: the historical training average projected as a constant. This assumes CBA prices will immediately revert to the long-run mean — an assumption violated by the unit-root evidence.





The three benchmark methods represent simple but important baselines. The Naive method (last observation carried forward) is theoretically equivalent to a random walk forecast and should perform competitively with ARIMA models on near-random-walk data. The Drift method extrapolates the historical average weekly change, capturing the training-period upward trend. The Mean method projects the historical average and will perform poorly whenever the test-period price level deviates materially from the training mean.


11.3 Exponential Smoothing (ETS)


Code
fc_ets_result <- ets_forecast(y_train, h = h)


Warning in ets(y_train): I can't handle data with frequency greater than 24.
Seasonality will be ignored. Try stlf() if you need seasonal forecasts.


Code

ETS model selected: ETS(M,N,N) 




Code
plot_forecast_simple(fc_ets_result$forecast, y_train, y_test,
                     model_name = paste0("ETS(", fc_ets_result$model$method, ")"))








Figure 42: ETS forecast with automatically selected error, trend, and seasonal components. ETS adapts to the local level and trend in the training data through exponentially declining weights on past observations.





Exponential Smoothing State Space (ETS) models assign exponentially declining weights to past observations, with the rate of decay governed by smoothing parameters. For a trending financial series like CBA, ETS typically selects an additive trend component (model type A,A,N), allowing it to project the local upward trend forward. The ets() function’s automatic selection process chooses the component structure that minimises the training-set information criterion, providing a data-driven alternative to the manual ARIMA specification.


11.4 Automatic ARIMA


Code
fc_auto_result <- auto_arima_forecast(y_train, h = h)
cat("auto.arima selected:", as.character(fc_auto_result$model), "\n")


auto.arima selected: ARIMA(2,1,2) with drift 




Code

                     model_name = paste0("auto.arima: ",
                                         as.character(fc_auto_result$model)))








Figure 43: auto.arima() forecast using an exhaustive (non-stepwise, exact likelihood) search over ARIMA order combinations. This serves as an algorithmic benchmark against the manually specified ARIMA(3,1,3).





auto.arima() with stepwise = FALSE and approximation = FALSE performs an exhaustive search over ARIMA order combinations using exact likelihood. Comparing its selected order against the manually specified ARIMA(3,1,3) validates the model specification process: if auto.arima() selects the same model, the manual analysis is confirmed; if it selects a different specification, the discrepancy warrants investigation.


11.5 TBATS


Code
fc_tbats_result <- tbats_forecast(y_train, h = h)
cat("TBATS model fitted successfully.\n")


TBATS model fitted successfully.




Code
plot_forecast_simple(fc_tbats_result$forecast, y_train, y_test, "TBATS")








Figure 44: TBATS forecast — handles complex seasonal patterns including multiple seasonal periods. For weekly financial data with frequency = 52, TBATS may capture annual trading cycles that ARIMA cannot model.





TBATS (Trigonometric, Box-Cox, ARMA errors, Trend, Seasonal) decomposes the series into trend, seasonal, and error components, handling multiple seasonal periods and non-integer frequencies. For CBA weekly data with frequency = 52, TBATS can model any annual trading cycle (e.g., year-end institutional rebalancing) that ARIMA treats as noise. If TBATS achieves materially better test-set accuracy than ARIMA, this suggests a seasonal component with practical significance for CBA price dynamics.

Figure 45: All forecasting methods overlaid for direct visual comparison. The red dashed line shows actual test-set prices. Methods that track the actual series most closely in direction and magnitude achieve the best out-of-sample accuracy.





Figure 45 provides the definitive visual comparison of all methods against the actual test-set prices. Methods that diverge substantially from the actual series — particularly any that project the training trend steeply upward while actual prices are flat or declining — are demonstrating over-reliance on historical momentum, which makes directional forecasting of near-random-walk equity prices so difficult. Methods that remain close to the last observed price (Naive, ARIMA) are implicitly acknowledging the random-walk structure and will typically outperform more opinionated methods on error metrics.
