title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
12 Conclusion
This analysis applied the full Box–Jenkins ARIMA workflow and a comprehensive suite of alternative forecasting methods to the weekly adjusted closing price of Commonwealth Bank of Australia (CBA.AX) over a five-year sample.
Transformation (rejected). The Box-Cox analysis (Figure 4) supported a log transformation statistically, but the applied transformation produced no material improvement in normality or variance stability relative to the original series (Figure 7, Figure 5). In accordance with parsimony, the original price series was retained for modelling.
Integration order (d = 1). The formal stationarity battery — ADF, PP, and KPSS — unanimously confirmed a unit root in the raw series (ADF p = 0.6649) and stationarity after one round of differencing. CBA’s price path incorporates each new shock — RBA decisions, global credit events, domestic housing data — permanently into the price level.
Model specification. The ACF/PACF (Figure 9), EACF, and dual BIC analysis (Figure 10, Figure 11) generated a candidate set spanning parsimonious low-order models through complex higher-order specifications. The diagnostic evidence consistently pointed toward a (3,1,3) or (4,1,3) structure, with the third and fourth lags carrying the primary autocorrelative signal.
Model selection. ARIMA(3,1,3) ML was selected as the final model on the convergence of three criteria: the lowest BIC (1360.501) across all candidate and augmented models, the best composite in-sample accuracy (ME = 0.3253, MAE = 2.3748, MASE = 0.1132), and the confirmation of white-noise residuals via Ljung-Box testing. The sparse structure — with insignificant intermediate lags (ar2/ma2) — reflects the genuine autocorrelative geometry of CBA weekly returns rather than model mis-specification. Although ARIMA(4,1,3) achieved a lower AIC, its marginal additional complexity was not rewarded by BIC or accuracy metrics, and the over-parameterisation check formally confirmed that the added parameter absorbs noise.
Residual adequacy. The ARIMA(3,1,3) specification passes the Ljung-Box white-noise criterion across all tested lags. Residual non-normality, attributable to extreme observations in 2025–2026, reflects the known fat-tail property of financial returns — a limitation of the Gaussian ARIMA framework addressed by GARCH extensions rather than higher ARIMA orders.
Forecasting. The multi-method comparison (Figure 45) demonstrates the fundamental difficulty of forecasting near-random-walk stock prices. Any method that does not substantially outperform the Naive benchmark on out-of-sample RMSE has not extracted exploitable information from the historical price series — an empirical confirmation of weak-form market efficiency for CBA.AX.
Limitations and extensions. CBA weekly returns exhibit volatility clustering visible in Figure 8 that no ARIMA model can capture. A GARCH(1,1) or EGARCH extension would model conditional heteroscedasticity and produce more accurate prediction intervals. Incorporating exogenous variables (ARIMAX) — the RBA cash rate, ASX 200 index, CBA’s net interest margin — would leverage macroeconomic theory to improve point forecast accuracy beyond what univariate price history provides.

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
9 Residual Diagnostics
The final preferred model is formally evaluated against the full residual diagnostic battery. A well-specified model should satisfy: (i) standardised residuals centred at zero with no systematic pattern; (ii) residuals approximating a Gaussian distribution (histogram, QQ plot, Shapiro-Wilk); (iii) ACF spikes entirely within the ±1.96/√n bounds; and (iv) Ljung-Box p-values consistently above 0.05 across all tested lags.


Code
# ── Replace model_313_cssml with your actual final model ──────────────────────
plot_residual_diagnostics(model_313_cssml, "ARIMA(3,1,3) CSS-ML", lag.max = 40)








Figure 36: Final model — six-panel residual diagnostic. Panels show standardised residuals over time, histogram, QQ plot, ACF of residuals, and Ljung-Box p-values.





Standardised residuals. [Note whether residuals are centred around zero with no visible trend or volatility clustering. Highlight any outlier clusters, e.g. in 2025–2026, and their likely cause.]
Histogram and QQ plot. [Note whether the distribution is approximately symmetric. Document any tail deviations from the Gaussian reference line. If heavy tails are present, attribute them to known market shock events.]
ACF of residuals. [State whether all spikes lie within the significance bands. A single marginally significant spike at one isolated lag is acceptable; systematic spikes suggest model under-specification.]
Shapiro-Wilk test. [State W statistic and p-value as printed during the diagnostic run. If p < 0.05, note that normality is rejected but that this reflects fat-tail financial return properties rather than mean-structure mis-specification.]
Ljung-Box p-values. [State whether all p-values exceed 0.05 across lags 1–40. If so, the residuals constitute white noise and the model’s mean structure is adequately specified.]
Overall verdict. [Summarise in two sentences: whether the model passes the diagnostic battery and whether any noted violations affect forecast validity.]

title: Forecasting ASX Stocks
text:
8 Overfitting Check
To confirm that the preferred candidate is not under-specified, we fit one-order augmentations and compare them on coefficient significance, residual diagnostics, information criteria, and accuracy.
Strategy: The preferred model is augmented by one additional AR or one additional MA parameter. If the new term is statistically insignificant and neither AIC/BIC nor accuracy improve, the preferred candidate is confirmed. If the new term is significant, the updated model supersedes the original and all information-criteria and accuracy tables are recomputed to include the augmented model.

# Replace the orders below to match your actual preferred candidate.
# Typical augmentation strategies (choose the one that makes sense):
#   If preferred = ARIMA(3,1,3)  → test ARIMA(4,1,3)  and/or ARIMA(3,1,4)
#   If preferred = ARIMA(4,1,3)  → test ARIMA(5,1,3)  and/or ARIMA(4,1,4)
#   If preferred = ARIMA(4,1,0)  → test ARIMA(5,1,0)
# ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

title: Forecasting Wool Trade
text:
1.1 Objectives
The specific objectives of this analysis are:

Descriptive Analysis: Visualise the monthly wool trade series (July 1971–February 2026) and compute summary statistics to identify trend, seasonality, and changing variance.
Data Transformation and Normality Assessment: Conduct an initial assessment of normality and evaluate the need for variance stabilisation. A Box-Cox transformation will be applied to determine the optimal power parameter only if required. Conduct formal testing for stationarity using the ADF test and ACF/PACF plots, implementing seasonal and non-seasonal differencing only as dictated by the diagnostic results.
Model Specification: Following the Box-Cox transformation to achieve normality and variance stabilisation, candidate Seasonal ARIMA (SARIMA) models are identified using a residual-based model specification approach. This iterative process, originally advocated by Box and Jenkins (1976), ensures the final model adequately captures the underlying data-generating process.
Parameter Estimates and Residual Diagnostics: Estimate all identified candidate SARIMA models using both Conditional Sum of Squares (CSS) and Maximum Likelihood (ML) methods. Following estimation, subject each candidate model to rigorous residual diagnostic evaluation to validate underlying assumptions. A model is considered adequate only if its residuals exhibit no significant autocorrelation (white noise) and an approximately normal distribution.
Model Selection: Among the adequate models, perform final selection using the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC), Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC), and in-sample accuracy metrics. The finalist model will be selected based on the lowest values across these information criteria, provided all parameter estimates are statistically significant and residual diagnostics are satisfied.
Overparameterisation: Once the top model is selected, perform an overparameterisation test by adding an extra seasonal or non-seasonal AR/MA term. This checks for overfitting and ensures parameter stability. If these extra terms are statistically insignificant, it proves the model is not overfitted, and the simpler, more parsimonious model is confirmed as the final winner.
Forecasting: The selected SARIMA model is used to generate 30-month out-of-sample forecasts with associated prediction intervals.

title: Forecasting Wool Trade
text:
1.2 Data Introduction


Code
Trade <- read.csv("International_Trade_in_Goods.csv", header = TRUE)


The dataset utilised in this study is sourced from the Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS), specifically Catalogue “5368.0: International Trade in Goods, Table 3: Goods Credits, Original, Current Prices”. The dataset encompasses a wide range of export categories, tracking monthly trade credits — economic transactions that result in money flowing into Australia from foreign buyers — across major rural and non-rural economic sectors. The data consist of 656 monthly observations from July 1971 to February 2026 and include 21 trade commodity categories.
To ensure the selected data would adequately support the deployment of the full spectrum of time-series modelling techniques covered this semester, an initial exploratory screening was conducted. Three candidate series were extracted for evaluation:

Wool & sheepskins (in AUD millions)
Metal ores & minerals (in AUD millions)
Meat & meat preparations (in AUD millions)

Table 1 presents the first five observations.

From the raw data, the original class was data frame and for the purpose of time series analysis, we will convert the data to a ts object with start = c(1971, 7), end = c(2026, 2), frequency = 12 (monthly data) to ensure compatibility with time series functions and maintain the temporal order of observations.
Although the dataset includes three commodity categories (wool, metal ores, and meat), this analysis focuses exclusively on wool and sheepskins for two reasons:

Economic significance: Wool has historically been one of Australia’s most valuable agricultural exports (National Museum Australia, 2025), contributing over AU$3.5 billion annually to the national economy.
Methodological interest: The wool trade is known to exhibit complex dynamics, including the structural break from the 1991 Reserve Price Scheme collapse, making it suitable for time series modelling.



Code
WoolTS <- ts(
  Trade$Wool...sheepskins..in..M.,
  start     = c(1971, 7),
  end       = c(2026, 2),
  frequency = 12
)

title: Forecasting Wool Trade
text:
2.1 Time Series Plot


Code
plot(WoolTS, type = "l", ylab = "AUD (Million)", xlab = "Year", cex.main = 0.9,
     main = "Wool & sheepskins monthly trade value (AUD million), 1971–2026.")

month_num <- cycle(WoolTS)
text(x = time(WoolTS), y = WoolTS, labels = month_num, cex = 0.5)








Figure 1: Wool & sheepskins monthly trade value (AUD million), 1971–2026.





Figure 1 shows the time series plot of the wool & sheepskins monthly trade value. Several features stand out:

Trend. The series exhibits a pronounced stochastic upward trend from 1971 through the late 1980s, peaking around 1988–1989, followed by a prolonged decline through the early 1990s, after which the series settles into a lower but more stable level, with a secondary elevation visible around 2018–2021.
Seasonality: Regular within-year oscillations are visible throughout the entire sample. December (12) frequently coincides with peak trade values, while August (8) is commonly observed at low points. These recurring annual cycles confirm that a SARIMA model with seasonal period \(s = 12\) months is appropriate.
Non-constant variance: The amplitude of fluctuations is substantially larger during the 1985–1991 commodity boom than in the surrounding decades. This heteroscedasticity suggests that a variance-stabilising transformation should be applied before modelling.
Change Point: The series exhibits a stark structural intervention around February 1991, representing the catastrophic collapse of the Australian Wool Corporation’s Reserve Price Scheme. This intervention permanently altered the long-term trend line.
Autocorrelation: The series shows strong autocorrelation, with consecutive monthly values tending to move together, which will be formally assessed using ACF/PACF plots.

title: Forecasting Wool Trade
text:
2.3 Distributional Diagnostics


Code
plot_norm(WoolTS,
          qq_title   = "Q-Q plot: original series.",
          hist_title = "Histogram: original series.")








Figure 2: Q-Q plot (left) and histogram (right) of the original series.







Code
sw_orig <- run_sw(WoolTS, "Shapiro-Wilk normality test: original series.")
sw_orig$table




Table 3: Shapiro-Wilk normality test: original series.




Shapiro-Wilk normality test: original series.


W statistic
p-value




0.9682
9.88e-11








Figure 2 and Table 3 confirm distributional non-normality. The Q-Q plot shows pronounced upper-tail deviation from the reference line. The histogram displays a unimodal but right-skewed distribution. The Shapiro-Wilk test yields \(W = 0.9682\) with \(p = 9.88e-11\); since this is below \(\alpha = 0.05\), we reject the null hypothesis of normality. This, combined with the visual evidence of heteroscedasticity, motivates applying a variance-stabilising power transformation before proceeding.

title: Forecasting Wool Trade
text:
2.4 Autocorrelation Function (ACF) & Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF)


Code
plot_seasonal_acf_pacf(
  WoolTS,
  acf_title  = "ACF: original series.",
  pacf_title = "PACF: original series."
)








Figure 3: ACF (left) and PACF (right) of the original series.





For this monthly data, lags are expressed in years (e.g., lag 1 = 12 months). Three features stand out in Figure 3:

Non-stationarity (slow ACF decay). The ACF decays very slowly — the canonical signature of a non-stationary mean.
AR(1)-type partial autocorrelation. The PACF exhibits a dominant spike at lag 1 followed by an abrupt cutoff.
Seasonal spikes. Elevated ACF and PACF values at lags 12, 24, and 36 (highlighted in red) confirm a significant seasonal periodic component at \(s = 12\) months.



Code
adf_orig <- run_adf(WoolTS, "Augmented Dickey-Fuller test: original series.")
adf_orig$table




Table 4: Augmented Dickey-Fuller test: original series.




Augmented Dickey-Fuller test: original series.


Dickey-Fuller statistic
Lags used
p-value




-3.3357
8
6.45e-02








The ADF test (Table 4) returns a Dickey-Fuller statistic of -3.3357 with \(p = 6.45e-02\). Because this p-value exceeds 0.05, we fail to reject the null hypothesis of a unit root, statistically confirming the series is non-stationary and that differencing will be required.

title: Forecasting Wool Trade
text:
3.1 Optimal \(\lambda\) Selection


Code
BC           <- BoxCox.ar(y = WoolTS, lambda = seq(-2, 2, 0.01),
                          method = "yule-walker")
lambda_opt   <- BC$lambda[which.max(BC$loglike)]
lambda_lower <- BC$ci[1]
lambda_upper <- BC$ci[3]








Figure 4: Box-Cox log-likelihood profile for optimal lambda selection.







Code
BC_WoolTS <- (WoolTS^lambda_opt - 1) / lambda_opt

knitr::kable(
  data.frame(
    Parameter = c("Optimal Lambda", "95% CI lower bound", "95% CI upper bound"),
    Value     = round(c(lambda_opt, lambda_lower, lambda_upper), 4)),
  align = "lc", row.names = FALSE, booktabs = TRUE)




Table 5: Box-Cox parameter estimates for the wool trade series.






Parameter
Value




Optimal Lambda
0.34


95% CI lower bound
0.23


95% CI upper bound
NA








Table 5 shows the optimal \(\lambda\) = 0.34, with a 95% confidence interval of [0.23, NA]. The upper bound could not be reliably estimated, but the lower bound confirms that \(\lambda = 1\) (no transformation) lies outside the interval. The estimated \(\lambda = 0.34\) is closer to a cube-root transformation (\(\lambda = 1/3\)) than to the more common log (\(\lambda = 0\)) or square-root (\(\lambda = 0.5\)) transformations.

        main = "Box-Cox transformed series, 1971–2026.",

The Shapiro-Wilk test yields W = 0.98 with p = 2.53e-07 (< 0.05), leading to rejection of normality. Despite this, the transformation has improved the distributional characteristics. Although SARIMA modelling does not strictly require data normality, the Box-Cox transformation significantly improved the series’ stationarity and variance characteristics. We therefore proceed with the transformed data for the remaining analysis.

plot_seasonal_acf_pacf(
  BC_WoolTS,

  pacf_title = "PACF: Box-Cox transformed.")

After Box-Cox transformation, both the non-seasonal unit root (slow ACF decay) and the seasonal unit root (spikes at lags 1, 2, and 3 corresponding to 12, 24, and 36 months) still remain. The PACF shows a dominant spike at lag 1 followed by a sharp cutoff, suggesting an AR(1) structure is required after differencing.

4.1 Step 1 — Seasonal Differencing: SARIMA\((0,0,0)(0,1,0)_{12}\)


Code
m1_wool <- Arima(BC_WoolTS, order = c(0, 0, 0),
                 seasonal = list(order = c(0, 1, 0), period = 12))
res_m1  <- rstandard(m1_wool)

plot(res_m1, type = "l", cex = 0.4, pch = 16, cex.main = 0.9,
     xlab = "Year", ylab = "Standardised residuals",
     main = "Residuals after fitting Model 1: SARIMA(0,0,0)(0,1,0)[12]")
abline(h = c(0, 2, -2), col = c("grey50", "red", "red"), lty = c(2, 3, 3))
legend("topright", legend = c("Residuals", "±2SD"),
       col = c("black", "red"), lty = c(1, 3), cex = 0.7, bty = "n")








Figure 8: Residuals after fitting Model 1: SARIMA(0,0,0)(0,1,0)[12].







Code
plot_seasonal_acf_pacf(
  res_m1,
  acf_title  = "ACF: Residuals, Model 1: SARIMA(0,0,0)(0,1,0)[12]",
  pacf_title = "PACF: Residuals, Model 1: SARIMA(0,0,0)(0,1,0)[12]")








Figure 9: ACF (left) and PACF (right) of residuals from Model 1: SARIMA(0,0,0)(0,1,0)[12].





Applying one seasonal difference (\(D = 1\)) to the Box-Cox transformed series removes much of the seasonal oscillation, as evidenced by the roughly zero-centred path in Figure 8. Even so, seasonal differencing alone is insufficient to fully eliminate the seasonal dependence, meaning seasonal ARMA terms are required to capture the remaining structure.
Looking at the diagnostics, the ACF in Figure 9 shows significant seasonal spikes at lags 12 and 24 remaining well above the significance bands, while the PACF shows slowly decaying seasonal spikes. Consequently, we choose seasonal moving average order \(Q = 2\) and seasonal autoregressive order \(P = 0\).

title: Forecasting Wool Trade
text:
4.2 Step 2 — Seasonal ARMA Specification: SARIMA\((0,0,0)(0,1,2)_{12}\)
The seasonal ACF in Figure 9 showed two significant spikes at seasonal lags 1 and 2 (corresponding to 12 and 24 months), which is the signature of a seasonal MA(2) process. The PACF showed slowly decaying seasonal lags, ruling out a seasonal AR component (\(P = 0\)). Accordingly, SMA(2) terms are added, fixing the seasonal structure at \((0,1,2)_{12}\) for all subsequent models. This give us: SARIMA\((0,0,0)(0,1,2)_{12}\).


Code
m2_wool <- Arima(BC_WoolTS, order = c(0, 0, 0),
                 seasonal = list(order = c(0, 1, 2), period = 12))
res_m2  <- rstandard(m2_wool)

plot(res_m2, type = "l", cex = 0.4, pch = 16, cex.main = 0.9,
     xlab = "Year", ylab = "Standardised residuals",
     main = "Residuals after fitting Model 2: SARIMA(0,0,0)(0,1,2)[12]")
abline(h = c(0, 2, -2), col = c("grey50", "red", "red"), lty = c(2, 3, 3))
legend("topright", legend = c("Residuals", "±2SD"),
       col = c("black", "red"), lty = c(1, 3), cex = 0.7, bty = "n")








Figure 10: Residuals after fitting Model 2: SARIMA(0,0,0)(0,1,2)[12].





After fitting Model 2, SARIMA\((0,0,0)(0,1,2)_{12}\), Figure 10 shows residuals are better behaved. Nevertheless, the footprint of historical shocks remains prominent, with extreme excursions still reaching the ±3 standard deviation threshold. Outside these extreme period, most standardised residuals lie within the ±2 standard deviation bounds.


Code
plot_seasonal_acf_pacf(
  res_m2,
  acf_title  = "ACF: Residuals, Model 2: SARIMA(0,0,0)(0,1,2)[12]",
  pacf_title = "PACF: Residuals, Model 2: SARIMA(0,0,0)(0,1,2)[12]")








Figure 11: ACF (left) and PACF (right) of residuals from Model 2: SARIMA(0,0,0)(0,1,2)[12].





The ACF in Figure 11 confirms the seasonal spikes at lags 12 and 24 now fall within the confidence bands — the SMA(2) term has absorbed the remaining seasonal autocorrelation. The seasonal component is therefore fixed at \((0,1,2)_{12}\) for all subsequent candidate models.
However, the ordinary (non-seasonal) lags reveal remaining short-term structure: the ACF shows slow decay and the PACF shows a dominant spike at ordinary lag 1 followed by a cutoff, indicating non-seasonal autocorrelation is still present. This suggests that non-seasonal differencing \((d=1)\) is required to stabilize the mean. So we run formal stationarity tests (Table 8) to confirm this.


Code
adf_resm2 <- check_stationarity(
  res_m2,
  "Formal statistical verification of stationarity for residuals of Model 2."
)

When running formal statistical tests to confirm the stationarity of the series, the result (Table 8) presents a classic contradiction. The ADF and PP tests reject the unit root hypothesis (indicating stationarity), while the KPSS test rejects the null of stationarity (indicating non-stationarity). Based on the persistent decay in the ACF plot, the sharp cutoff in the PACF, and the KPSS test confirming non-stationarity in the levels, a first-difference \(d = 1\) conservatively, establishing the baseline structural framework as SARIMA\((p,1,q)(0,1,2)_{12}\).

title: Forecasting Wool Trade
text:
4.3 Step 3 — Non-Seasonal Differencing & ARMA Diagnostics
Based on the contradictory stationarity test results from Step 2 and the visual evidence of remaining non-seasonal autocorrelation, a non-seasonal difference (\(d = 1\)) is applied conservatively. This produces the baseline structural framework SARIMA\((0,1,0)(0,1,2)_{12}\), from which all subsequent candidate models will be built.


Code
m3_wool <- Arima(BC_WoolTS, order = c(0, 1, 0),
                 seasonal = list(order = c(0, 1, 2), period = 12))
res_m3  <- rstandard(m3_wool)

plot(res_m3, type = "l", cex = 0.4, pch = 16, cex.main = 0.9,
     xlab = "Year", ylab = "Standardised residuals",
     main = "Residuals after fitting Model 3: SARIMA(0,1,0)(0,1,2)[12]")
abline(h = c(0, 2, -2), col = c("grey50", "red", "red"), lty = c(2, 3, 3))
legend("topright", legend = c("Residuals", "±2SD"),
       col = c("black", "red"), lty = c(1, 3), cex = 0.7, bty = "n")








Figure 12: Residuals after fitting Model 3: SARIMA(0,1,0)(0,1,2)[12].





Figure 12 shows the standardised residuals fluctuating randomly around zero with no remaining trend or seasonal pattern, confirming that (\(d = 1\)) has successfully stabilised the mean. Most residuals lie within the ±2 bounds. A few extreme spikes exceeding ±3 are visible during known historical shock periods, specifically the volatile macro environments of the early 1980s and the 2020–2022 global disruptions. Outside these isolated episodes, variance clustering is negligible, pointing to a stable conditional variance.


Code
plot_seasonal_acf_pacf(
  res_m3,
  acf_title  = "ACF: Residuals, Model 3: SARIMA(0,1,0)(0,1,2)[12]",
  pacf_title = "PACF: Residuals, Model 3: SARIMA(0,1,0)(0,1,2)[12]")








Figure 13: ACF (left) and PACF (right) of residuals from Model 3: SARIMA(0,1,0)(0,1,2)[12].





Figure Figure 13 presents the ACF and PACF of the residuals for Model 3 SARIMA\((0,1,0)(0,1,2)_{12}\). The dominant seasonal spikes have been removed, confirming that the seasonal structure \((0,1,2)_{12}\) and the non-seasonal differencing (\(d = 1\)) have together absorbed seasonal and trend-related autocorrelation.
However, a detailed inspection of the ordinary (non-seasonal) lags reveals remaining short-term dependence, confirming that p and q are not yet specified. Both the ACF and PACF in Figure 13 show approximately 3 significant spikes at ordinary lags, after which correlations fall within the confidence bands, giving a preliminary indication that both \(p\) and \(q\) are in the range of 1–3.
Consequently, the Model 3 residuals, with seasonal structure, seasonal autocorrelation (\(P = 0, D = 1, Q = 2\)) , and non-seasonal trend (\(d = 1\)) all removed, now contain only the short-term non-seasonal ARMA(p,q) dependence. These residuals are therefore the correct and necessary input for the formal order identification tools for Step 5.

title: Forecasting Wool Trade
text:
4.4 Step 4 — Full Model Autocorrelation Check
To validate that the SARIMA\((p,1,q)(0,1,2)_{12}\) framework is sufficient to fully capture all remaining autocorrelation, Model 4, SARIMA\((2,1,2)(0,1,2)_{12}\), is fitted as a diagnostic trial, using (\(p = 2\)) and (\(q = 2\)) to directly address the approximate 3 significant spikes observed in both the ACF and PACF of the Model 3 residuals (Figure 13).


Code
m4_wool <- Arima(BC_WoolTS, order = c(2, 1, 2),
                 seasonal = list(order = c(0, 1, 2), period = 12))
res_m4  <- rstandard(m4_wool)

plot(res_m4, type = "l", cex = 0.4, pch = 16, cex.main = 0.9,
     xlab = "Year", ylab = "Standardised residuals",
     main = "Residuals after fitting Model 4: SARIMA(2,1,2)(0,1,2)[12]")
abline(h = c(0, 2, -2), col = c("grey50", "red", "red"), lty = c(2, 3, 3))
legend("topright", legend = c("Residuals", "±2SD"),
       col = c("black", "red"), lty = c(1, 3), cex = 0.7, bty = "n")








Figure 14: Residuals after fitting Model 4: SARIMA(2,1,2)(0,1,2)[12].







Code
plot_seasonal_acf_pacf(
  res_m4,
  acf_title  = "ACF: Residuals, Model 4: SARIMA(2,1,2)(0,1,2)[12]",
  pacf_title = "PACF: Residuals, Model 4: SARIMA(2,1,2)(0,1,2)[12]")








Figure 15: ACF (left) and PACF (right) of residuals from Model 4: SARIMA(2,1,2)(0,1,2)[12].





Figure 14 shows the residuals fluctuating randomly around zero within the ±2 bounds. The absence of long-wave patterns or cyclical drift confirms that the non-seasonal ARIMA(2,1,2) parameters have successfully filtered out remaining short-term dependencies, leaving residuals that closely resemble white noise.
Figure 15 confirms the absence of any systematic patterns or persistent autocorrelation at either ordinary or seasonal lags, indicating that the residuals have been successfully reduced to white noise. This validates that the SARIMA\((p,1,q)(0,1,2)_{12}\) framework is sufficient to fully capture the series structure.

title: Forecasting Wool Trade
text:
4.5 Step 5 — Non-Seasonal ARMA Order Identification
Following the elimination of all seasonal dynamics and non-seasonal trends, the Model 3 residuals contain only the isolated, pure non-seasonal ARMA(p,q) signal. To identify the optimal order for this remaining process, we deploy three complementary identification tools, beginning with the visual diagnostics provided by the (Model 3) residual ACF and PACF plots in Figure 13.

4.5.1 ACF & PACF of the Model 3 Residuals
The ACF and PACF of the Model 3 residuals, already presented in Figure 13, provide the preliminary visual basis for identifying candidate non-seasonal orders. The ACF shows approximately 4 significant spikes at ordinary lags, indicating \(q\) in the range of 1–3. The PACF shows approximately 3 significant spikes, indicating \(p\) in the range of 1–3. This gives 9 ACF/PACF-derived candidate structures covering all combinations of \(p \in \{1,2,3\}\) and \(q \in \{1,2,3\}\).
Combining these ranges gives 9 ACF/PACF-derived candidate structures:

SARIMA\((1,1,1)(0,1,2)_{12}\)
SARIMA\((1,1,2)(0,1,2)_{12}\)
SARIMA\((1,1,3)(0,1,2)_{12}\)
SARIMA\((2,1,1)(0,1,2)_{12}\)
SARIMA\((2,1,2)(0,1,2)_{12}\)
SARIMA\((2,1,3)(0,1,2)_{12}\)
SARIMA\((3,1,1)(0,1,2)_{12}\)
SARIMA\((3,1,2)(0,1,2)_{12}\)
SARIMA\((3,1,3)(0,1,2)_{12}\)



4.5.2 EACF Analysis of the Model 3 Residuals
However, as established in Step 3, the presence of spikes in both plots simultaneously indicates a mixed ARMA process where exact orders cannot be determined by visual inspection alone. EACF and BIC are required.
The EACF is applied to the residuals of Model 3 SARIMA\((0,1,0)(0,1,2)_{12}\). Because Model 3 has already filtered out the seasonal structure \((0,1,2)_{12}\) and non-seasonal trend \((d=1)\) have already been filtered, the remaining information in these residuals belongs entirely to the short-term non-seasonal ARMA(p,q) process, making them the correct input for order identification.


Code
eacf_res <- eacf(res_m3, ar.max = 7, ma.max = 13)

The EACF is read by locating the upper-left vertex of the triangle of ‘o’ values. The first stable triangle has its vertex at AR=0, MA=2, identifying ARMA(0,2) as the most parsimonious candidate. The ‘o’ pattern also holds at AR=0, MA=3; AR=1, MA=2; and AR=1, MA=3. These four EACF-derived candidates correspond to

SARIMA\((0,1,2)(0,1,2)_{12}\)
SARIMA\((0,1,3)(0,1,2)_{12}\)
SARIMA\((1,1,2)(0,1,2)_{12}\)
SARIMA\((1,1,3)(0,1,2)_{12}\)



4.5.3 BIC Subset-Selection Plot of the Model 3 Residuals
To complement the EACF analysis, BIC subset-selection plots were generated to identify the most promising non-seasonal ARMA orders. These plots display BIC values for different combinations of AR(p) and MA(q) orders, with darker cells indicating lower(BIC) values. Figure 16 and 17 present the results for two different search ranges (nar = 10, nma = 10, and nar =5, nma = 5)


Code
res_bic <- armasubsets(y = res_m3, nar = 10, nma = 10,
                       y.name = "res", ar.method = "ols")

Figure 16: BIC subset-selection plot (AR max = 10, MA max = 10).





The BIC subset-selection plots are generated across two search ranges to assess stability of the results. The wider search selects high MA orders up to lag 8, which are theoretically implausible for this series and likely reflect overfitting across the larger search space. These results are therefore not carried forward as candidates.

                        y.name = "res", ar.method = "ols")

The narrower search, on the other hand, produces more reliable and practically meaningful results. The best model includes AR lag 1 and MA lag 3, corresponding to ARMA(1,3) giving SARIMA\((1,1,3)(0,1,2)_{12}\) as the primary BIC-derived candidate. The second row includes AR lag 2 and MA lag 2 and 5, corresponding to ARMA(2,2) and ARMA(2,5). But ARMA(2,5) is not carried forward as a candidate. BIC-derived candidates are therefore:

SARIMA\((1,1,3)(0,1,2)_{12}\)
SARIMA\((2,1,2)(0,1,2)_{12}\)

7.1.1 Synthesis: Information Criteria Comparison
Across both information criteria, the evidence consistently favours M311 among viable candidates with a sufficiently rich non-seasonal structure. M213 is disqualified on structural grounds despite its nominal AIC lead. M012 and M111 lead BIC through parsimony alone but lack the autoregressive depth required to adequately capture short-run dynamics. M311 therefore emerges as the primary finalist on both AIC and BIC among structurally adequate models, with M212 retained as the parsimonious runner-up and M111 as the strictest-parsimony alternative.
Information criteria provide a theoretical comparison. The next section examines in-sample accuracy (RMSE, MAE, MAPE, MASE) to further confirm the selection of M311.

title: Forecasting Wool Trade
text:
7.3 Finalist Model
Three sequential filters have been applied — parameter estimation stability and residual diagnostics, information criteria (AIC and BIC), and in-sample accuracy metrics — and all three consistently point to the same conclusion. SARIMA\((3,1,1)(0,1,2)_{12}\) (M311) is selected as the final model.
Across all three filters, M311 is the only viable candidate that leads consistently without a disqualifying failure on any dimension. Under ML and CSS-ML, it presents the strongest coefficient profile in the candidate set with all non-seasonal parameters achieving significance. It ranks first on AIC (1855.1) and first on BIC (1886.4) among structurally adequate candidates with comparable non-seasonal complexity.
It (M311) leads on every primary in-sample accuracy metric — RMSE (38.923), MAE (28.160), MAPE (12.300), and MASE (0.511). The near-unit ma1 (|ma1| = 0.9883) is the sole acknowledged concern: it sits in proximity to the invertibility boundary but does not cross it, and is outweighed by the model’s consistent superiority across all three evaluation filters.

title: Forecasting Wool Trade
text:
8.1 Overfit by q+1 — M312: SARIMA\((3,1,2)(0,1,2)_{12}\)
M312 was already estimated. Adding a second MA term to the selected M311 causes a complete collapse of the entire non-seasonal structure under both CSS and ML estimation. All five non-seasonal parameters, ar1, ar2, ar3, ma1, and ma2, are entirely insignificant across both methods.
The model is effectively reduced to a pure seasonal structure driven solely by sma1, with no non-seasonal dynamics whatsoever, a severe regression from M311’s well-identified AR(3) and MA(1) framework. AIC worsens from 1855.107 to 1865.638 (+10.531 units) and BIC worsens from 1886.370 to 1901.367 (+14.997 units). Residual diagnostics show no improvement over M311. Adding further complexity beyond M311 adds no explanatory value and actively degrades model quality.
With the q+1 path definitively ruled out due to structural collapse, the final component of the overfitting verification requires testing the p+1 direction. Accordingly, the SARIMA\((4,1,1)(0,1,2)_{12}\) model will be estimated next. Its resulting coefficient significance and residual diagnostics will be analyzed to confirm whether expanding the autoregressive structure yields any genuine predictive power.

title: Forecasting Wool Trade
text:
8.3 Overfitting Decision
Both overfitting directions fail conclusively. The q+1 extension (M312) produces complete non-seasonal coefficient collapse with AIC worsening by 10.531 units and BIC by 14.997 units. The p+1 extension (M411) produces an identical collapse under both CSS and ML, with all five non-seasonal parameters insignificant across both estimation methods. Neither direction of extension adds any value beyond M311. SARIMA\((3,1,1)(0,1,2)_{12}\) is confirmed as the final selected model.
Having confirmed the final model, we now generate 30-month-ahead forecasts to illustrate its practical application.

title: So What?
text:
Australia has long been the world’s dominant supplier of fine wool. Today, wool and sheepskin exports contribute more than AU$3.5 billion annually to the national economy, sustaining thousands of farming families across New South Wales, Victoria, and Western Australia. But beneath that headline figure lies a commodity story defined by dramatic boom-and-bust cycles, structural shocks, and persistent seasonal rhythms that have shaped, and at times devastated, the industry.
The most defining moment in modern wool market history came in February 1991, when the Australian Wool Corporation’s Reserve Price Scheme collapsed. For years, the scheme had artificially propped up wool prices by guaranteeing a floor price and stockpiling surplus product. When the stockpile became financially unsustainable, the scheme was abandoned overnight. Prices fell by nearly 50% within months. Farmers destocked, regional communities contracted, and the industry spent most of the 1990s in structural decline. This single event permanently altered the long-term trajectory of Australia’s wool trade — a “before and after” line that any serious analysis of the data must account for.
Within that broader arc, the wool trade also follows a deeply seasonal rhythm. Australia’s wool clip, the annual shearing cycle, drives predictable surges in export activity during the December–March period as freshly shorn wool moves through the supply chain. Trade values reliably trough around August, before recovering through the second half of the year. This within-year pattern has held consistently for decades, making seasonality one of the most exploitable structural features of the series for forecasting purposes.

title: So What?
section: 2 What Is SARIMA, and Why Use It Here?
text:
2 What Is SARIMA, and Why Use It Here?
The Seasonal Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average model — SARIMA — is one of the most well-established statistical frameworks for forecasting time series data that exhibits both trend and seasonality. To understand why it fits the wool trade so well, it helps to unpack what the model is actually doing.
A time series like monthly wool export values carries memory. This month’s trade value is correlated with last month’s, and with the same month last year. That memory has two distinct components: short-run momentum (how recent values influence the immediate next observation) and seasonal cyclicality (how the same calendar period behaves year after year). SARIMA captures both simultaneously.
The “Integrated” component handles the trend problem. Raw wool export values are non-stationary — they drift upward and downward over decades, making direct statistical modelling unreliable. Differencing the series (subtracting each observation from the one before it) removes that drift and converts the series into stationary fluctuations around a stable mean. One regular difference handles the trend; one seasonal difference removes the annual cycle’s mean-shifting component.
The “Autoregressive” and “Moving Average” components then model what remains: the short-run correlation structure of those stationary fluctuations. Autoregressive terms capture the influence of past values on the current value — the momentum effect. Moving average terms capture the lingering influence of past shocks — how an unexpected spike or trough in one month echoes through the subsequent months before fading.
Before any of this modelling could proceed, a Box-Cox transformation was applied to the raw data. The amplitude of wool price fluctuations was dramatically larger during the 1985–1991 commodity boom than in surrounding decades, a classic case of heteroscedasticity, where the variance of the series changes over time. The transformation (with an estimated power parameter of λ = 0.34, close to a cube-root transformation) compressed the larger fluctuations and stabilised the variance, ensuring that the model wasn’t disproportionately fitted to the most volatile era at the expense of the rest of the sample.
After this sequential process of transformation, differencing, and model identification through ACF, PACF, EACF, and BIC analysis, eleven candidate SARIMA models were evaluated. The final selected model, SARIMA(3,1,1)(0,1,2)₁₂, captures three months of autoregressive momentum, one moving average shock term, one seasonal difference, and two seasonal moving average terms operating at a 12-month period. All parameters are statistically significant, residuals behave as white noise, and the model passes overfitting verification: neither adding a fourth AR term nor a second MA term improves the model — in fact, both directions collapse the non-seasonal structure entirely, confirming that the three-AR specification is precisely sufficient.

title: So What?
section: 3 So What? Translating the Forecast into Decisions
text:
3 So What? Translating the Forecast into Decisions
This is where the analysis moves from statistical exercise to operational value. The 30-month forecast generated by the final model projects a characteristic seasonal pattern repeating coherently across 2026–2028: a peak around March (approximately AU$385 million in March 2026, declining modestly to AU$358 million by March 2028), a mid-year trough around August (AU$206 million in 2026), and a secondary recovery toward December (AU$313 million). Point forecasts show a gentle downward drift across the horizon, with widening 95% prediction intervals that honestly reflect compounding uncertainty over time.
For wool producers, this pattern directly aligns with biological decision timelines. Shearing decisions, stocking rate adjustments, and forward contract negotiations happen 6–12 months before the fleece actually moves. Having a statistically grounded forecast of the trade value trajectory — rather than relying on last season’s prices or industry sentiment — allows producers to time their clip sales into projected demand peaks and hedge against the mid-year trough. The model’s gradual downward drift across the forecast horizon is itself a signal worth heeding: it suggests that simply holding product in expectation of ongoing price strength may not be the optimal strategy.
For exporters and logistics firms, the December demand surge — visible and quantifiable across all three forecast years — provides lead time that has direct commercial value. Ocean freight container bookings on the Australia–China and Australia–Europe wool corridors need to be secured well in advance of peak periods. Cold store and wool store warehouse capacity in major port cities similarly requires forward planning. The SARIMA forecast converts an historically “known but unmeasurable” seasonal surge into a specific, interval-bounded projection that can anchor capacity planning conversations with shipping lines and third-party logistics providers.
For agricultural policymakers and trade analysts, the widening prediction intervals are perhaps the most important output. The 95% interval for August 2026 trade values spans from AU$120 million to AU$327 million — a range of AU$207 million. By August 2028, that range widens to AU$305 million. These intervals are not a weakness of the model; they are an honest quantification of how uncertain the wool trade genuinely is two to three years out. Policy stress-testing that assumes the point forecast will materialise is systematically overconfident. Budget projections, export credit programs, and agricultural support mechanisms that don’t account for the lower bound of the forecast range are exposed to the same kind of tail-event risk that made the 1991 collapse so catastrophic.

title: So What?
section: 4 What the Model Cannot Do — and Why That Matters Too
text:
4 What the Model Cannot Do — and Why That Matters Too
A SARIMA framework is powerful precisely because it does one thing very well: it extracts and projects the patterns already embedded in the historical data. It cannot anticipate a future trade policy shock, a new entrant disrupting the global fine wool supply chain, or a repeat of a pandemic-scale demand collapse. The structural break of 1991 is a permanent feature of this dataset; the model accounts for its aftermath but cannot have predicted it in advance.
This limitation is not a reason to dismiss the forecast — it is a reason to use it correctly. The SARIMA output is most valuable as a baseline scenario: what happens if the current structural regime continues and no major external shock occurs. Deviations from that baseline — when actual trade data begins arriving each month — are themselves informative signals. A sustained underperformance relative to the forecast may indicate an emerging structural shift that warrants intervention. An overperformance may indicate unmodelled demand drivers worth investigating.
The analysis also conducted a separate post-break sub-series analysis (1990–2026) that identified a structurally different SARIMA(0,1,2)(2,1,2)₁₂ model for the modern era — confirming that the data-generating processes before and after the Reserve Price Scheme collapse are genuinely distinct. For near-term decision-making, this post-break model may offer more relevant signal, since it is fitted exclusively to the market regime that currently exists.

title: So What?
text:
5 The Bottom Line
Fifty-five years of monthly wool trade data, processed through a rigorous statistical pipeline, converges on a clear message: Australian wool exports follow a predictable seasonal cycle with a gradual moderating trend, and that cycle can be quantified with enough precision to be operationally useful. The SARIMA(3,1,1)(0,1,2)₁₂ model doesn’t eliminate uncertainty — nothing can — but it replaces vague expectation with calibrated probability ranges that can anchor real decisions about stocking, shipping, warehousing, and policy. In a commodity sector where the difference between correctly timed and poorly timed decisions can span tens of millions of dollars, that is not a minor improvement. It is the difference between reactive management and genuine foresight.
